This date triggers the contractor's warranty period, retainage release, and the punch list process.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.9.8; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: A contractor submits a claim for additional compensation due to owner-caused delay. Under AIA A201, what is the time limit within which the contractor must provide initial written notice of a claim?
options: 30 days after the claimant first recognizes the condition
7 days after the claimant first recognizes the condition
21 days after the claimant first recognizes the condition
14 days after the claimant first recognizes the condition
correctOption: 21 days after the claimant first recognizes the condition
explanation: AIA A201 S.15.1.3 requires initial written notice of a claim within 21 days after occurrence of the event or after the claimant first recognizes the condition giving rise to the claim. Failure to provide timely notice may waive the right to the claim. This 21-day notice period is a critical contractual deadline.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.15.1.3; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: What is the primary purpose of a post-occupancy evaluation (POE)?
options: To audit the final construction cost against the original budget
To systematically evaluate building performance against design intent and occupant needs after move-in
To review the project for LEED certification compliance
To assess contractor warranty compliance one year after completion
correctOption: To systematically evaluate building performance against design intent and occupant needs after move-in
explanation: A Post-Occupancy Evaluation (POE) assesses how well a completed building performs from the occupants' perspective, comparing actual performance against design goals (energy use, comfort, functionality, spatial adequacy). POE findings inform future design improvements and are increasingly valued in evidence-based design.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines; AIA Framework for Design Excellence

question: The owner and contractor agree to extend the contract time because of an owner-approved scope change. Which AIA document is typically used to formalize the agreed change?
options: AIA G731 - Agreement Between Architect and Contractor's Surety
AIA G714 - Construction Change Directive
AIA G710 - Architect's Supplemental Instructions
AIA G701 - Change Order
correctOption: AIA G701 - Change Order
explanation: AIA G701 is used when the owner and contractor agree to a change in the work, contract sum, contract time, or a combination of these. A construction change directive may be used when work must proceed before agreement is reached.
codeReference: AIA G701-2017; AIA A201-2017 S.7.2; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: An owner terminates a contractor for cause under AIA A201. Which of the following is NOT a valid reason for termination for cause?
options: Disregard for applicable laws, statutes, or codes
Persistent failure to supply adequate skilled workers
The owner decides a different contractor would be less expensive
Failure to make payments to subcontractors
correctOption: The owner decides a different contractor would be less expensive
explanation: AIA A201 S.14.2 allows termination for cause when the contractor: fails to supply adequate workers; fails to pay subcontractors; disregards laws or codes; or otherwise substantially breaches the contract. Simply wanting a cheaper contractor is not cause for termination for cause - that would be termination for convenience (S.14.4), which requires paying the contractor for work done plus a reasonable overhead and profit on work not done.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.14.2, S.14.4; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: An architect observes construction progress weekly and notes the contractor is behind schedule. What is the architect's role?
options: Direct the contractor to work overtime to recover the schedule
Adjust the construction documents to simplify the remaining work
Report the schedule status to the owner and the contractor, but do not direct contractor means and methods
Terminate the contractor's contract on the owner's behalf
correctOption: Report the schedule status to the owner and the contractor, but do not direct contractor means and methods
explanation: The architect observes and reports - not directs construction means and methods. Per AIA A201 S.4.2, the architect administers the contract but the contractor has sole responsibility for means, methods, and sequences of construction. The architect informs the owner of schedule status; the owner may then exercise contractual rights to demand a recovery schedule from the contractor.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.4.2; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: What is the purpose of a 'field report' (site observation report) prepared by the architect?
options: To certify that all work in place is complete and acceptable
To authorize the contractor to proceed with the next phase of work
To formally reject nonconforming work on behalf of the owner
To document observed conditions, work in progress, and issues for the project record
correctOption: To document observed conditions, work in progress, and issues for the project record
explanation: An architect's field report (site observation report) documents what was observed during a site visit: work in progress, materials delivered, personnel on site, weather, and any issues or open items. These reports build a project record, communicate observations to the owner, and protect all parties if disputes arise later. They do not certify completion or approve the work.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.4.2; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines; AIA G711 (Architect's Field Report)

question: Under AIA A201, who has the authority to stop the work if a safety emergency exists on the construction site?
options: The architect, as the owner's representative
OSHA, under federal jurisdiction only
The owner's project manager
The contractor, who has sole responsibility for site safety
correctOption: The contractor, who has sole responsibility for site safety
explanation: AIA A201 S.10.1 assigns safety responsibility to the contractor, not the architect. The contractor is responsible for initiating, maintaining, and supervising all safety precautions and programs. The architect is not responsible for the contractor's safety programs and does not have authority to stop work for safety under AIA contracts (unless there is an imminent threat and the architect issues a stop-work notice as authorized by the owner).
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.10.1-10.3; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: What is 'retainage' in a construction contract, and when is it typically released?
options: A bond amount retained by the surety until the warranty period expires
A fee paid to the architect for construction administration; released at project close-out
The owner's contingency budget retained for unforeseen conditions
A percentage withheld from each contractor payment as security; released upon substantial or final completion
correctOption: A percentage withheld from each contractor payment as security; released upon substantial or final completion
explanation: Retainage is a percentage (typically 5-10%) withheld from each progress payment to ensure the contractor completes the work. It incentivizes contractor performance and provides owner security. Under AIA A201 S.9.8.5, retainage may be reduced upon substantial completion, with the balance released with final payment after all punch list items are resolved.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.9.3, S.9.8.5, S.9.10; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: What does AIA A201 require the contractor to provide before final payment is released?
options: A certificate from the architect confirming all work was acceptable
OSHA certification that the site is clean and safe
Lien waivers, consent of surety, and any required affidavits of payment
Building department certificate of occupancy only
correctOption: Lien waivers, consent of surety, and any required affidavits of payment
explanation: AIA A201 S.9.10 conditions final payment on the contractor providing: (1) an affidavit that all payrolls, bills for materials, and other liabilities have been paid; (2) consent of the surety to final payment; (3) a certificate of insurance for post-completion coverage if required; and (4) other data establishing that no liens have been filed. This protects the owner from mechanic's liens after final payment.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.9.10; AIA G706-2017; AIA G707-2017; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: A structural steel connection fails inspection because the welds do not meet AWS D1.1 standards. Which course of action is MOST appropriate per AIA A201?
options: The owner must accept the work as-is since the contractor has already been paid for it
The architect issues a Change Order increasing the contract sum to cover re-welding
The architect negotiates a price reduction with the contractor for the deficient welds
The architect rejects the work and requires the contractor to correct it at no additional cost to the owner
correctOption: The architect rejects the work and requires the contractor to correct it at no additional cost to the owner
explanation: Per AIA A201 S.4.2.6 and S.12.2, the architect has authority to reject nonconforming work. The contractor must correct defective work at no additional cost to the owner. AWS D1.1 is the specification standard referenced in the contract documents; failure to comply is a breach of contract. The architect's rejection initiates the correction process - no Change Order or price reduction is appropriate for the contractor's own deficient work.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.4.2.6, S.12.2; AWS D1.1; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: Which AIA document would an architect use to make a written interpretation or decision regarding a contract dispute between the owner and contractor?
options: AIA G701 - Change Order
AIA G704 - Certificate of Substantial Completion
A written Initial Decision per AIA A201 S.15.2
AIA G710 - Architect's Supplemental Instructions
correctOption: A written Initial Decision per AIA A201 S.15.2
explanation: When a dispute arises, AIA A201 S.15.2 establishes the architect as the Initial Decision Maker (IDM). The architect reviews claims from either party and renders an Initial Decision in writing within 10 days. Either party may reject the decision and demand mediation. The IDM role is quasi-judicial - the architect must act impartially, even toward the owner.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.15.2; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: What is the difference between 'substantial completion' and 'final completion' in a construction contract?
options: Substantial completion occurs when the work is usable for its intended purpose; final completion occurs when all punch list items are corrected and the architect issues the final certificate
Final completion occurs at substantial completion; the architect then has 30 days to issue final certificate
Substantial completion and final completion both occur on the same date
They are legally the same - both trigger the release of all retainage
correctOption: Substantial completion occurs when the work is usable for its intended purpose; final completion occurs when all punch list items are corrected and the architect issues the final certificate
explanation: Substantial Completion (AIA A201 S.9.8) is the milestone when the owner can use the building for its intended purpose - it triggers warranty start, insurance transfer, and reduced retainage. Final Completion (S.9.10) occurs when all punch list items are finished, documentation is submitted, and the architect issues the final Certificate for Payment releasing all remaining funds.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 S.9.8, S.9.10; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: A contractor proposes a substitute roof membrane product after construction has begun. What should the architect do first?
options: Require a formal substitution submittal with supporting data so the design team can evaluate compliance with the contract documents
Reject all substitutions without review
Tell the owner to negotiate directly with the roofing subcontractor
Accept the substitution if it is less expensive
correctOption: Require a formal substitution submittal with supporting data so the design team can evaluate compliance with the contract documents
explanation: Substitutions should be submitted through the contract process with product data, performance information, warranty implications, and cost or schedule effects. The architect evaluates whether the proposed product meets the design intent and contract requirements.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017 Article 3

question: A contractor's payment application includes stored materials located off-site. What documentation is most important before the architect recommends payment?
options: Evidence of ownership, insurance, storage location, and compliance with contract requirements
A revised zoning analysis
A verbal promise from the subcontractor
A photograph only
correctOption: Evidence of ownership, insurance, storage location, and compliance with contract requirements
explanation: Payment for stored materials can be appropriate if allowed by the contract, but the architect should require documentation such as bills of sale, proof of insurance, secure storage information, and evidence that the materials are properly identified for the project.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017 Article 9

question: The architect suspects that concealed work does not match the contract documents and requests that it be uncovered. If the uncovered work is found to be nonconforming, who typically pays for uncovering and correction?
options: The building official, because inspections were required
The architect, because the architect requested the uncovering
The owner, because the work was already covered
The contractor, because the work was nonconforming
correctOption: The contractor, because the work was nonconforming
explanation: Under typical AIA general conditions, if uncovered work is found not to comply with the contract documents, the contractor generally bears the cost of uncovering, correction, and recovery. If the work is conforming, cost allocation may be different.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017 Article 12

question: What is the purpose of a Certificate of Substantial Completion?
options: To certify that every punch list item is complete
To replace the certificate for payment
To release the contractor from all warranty obligations
To establish the date when the work or designated portion is sufficiently complete for the owner to occupy or use it, and to define remaining responsibilities
correctOption: To establish the date when the work or designated portion is sufficiently complete for the owner to occupy or use it, and to define remaining responsibilities
explanation: Substantial completion marks the point when the owner can occupy or use the work for its intended purpose. The certificate establishes the date, identifies remaining items, and can affect warranties, insurance, retainage, and responsibilities.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017 Article 9

question: When is a Construction Change Directive most useful?
options: When the architect wants to change the contract without the owner
When the owner and contractor agree on scope, price, and time before work starts
When the contractor wants to avoid documenting a change
When a change must proceed before the owner and contractor agree on the exact adjustment in contract sum or time
correctOption: When a change must proceed before the owner and contractor agree on the exact adjustment in contract sum or time
explanation: A construction change directive allows changed work to proceed when the parties have not yet agreed on cost or schedule adjustment. A change order is used when the owner and contractor agree on scope, contract sum, and contract time.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017 Article 7

question: During a site visit, the architect observes an apparently unsafe excavation condition. What is the most appropriate response under a typical owner-architect-contractor relationship?
options: Stop visiting the site until construction is complete
Direct the contractor's workers exactly how to shore the excavation
Promptly notify the contractor and owner of the observed concern without assuming control of means, methods, or safety programs
Call OSHA directly and provide them the contractor's contact information
correctOption: Promptly notify the contractor and owner of the observed concern without assuming control of means, methods, or safety programs
explanation: The contractor is typically responsible for construction means, methods, and safety. However, if the architect observes a serious safety concern, the prudent response is to promptly communicate the observation to the contractor and owner while avoiding direction of the contractor's safety procedures.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017 Article 3 and Article 4

question: During a site visit, the architect sees installed work that appears inconsistent with the contract documents. What should the architect do first?
options: Direct the subcontractor crew how to rebuild it
Document the observation and notify the contractor through the proper contract communication process
Approve the work because it is already installed
Issue an immediate stop-work order to the entire project
correctOption: Document the observation and notify the contractor through the proper contract communication process
explanation: The architect should document observed nonconforming work and communicate through the contract process without taking over means and methods.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor submits a pay application including work not yet installed or stored. What should the architect do?
options: Base certification solely on the subcontractor invoice total
Certify the full amount to preserve goodwill
Defer all payment review until final completion
Recommend the amount properly supported by completed or stored work
correctOption: Recommend the amount properly supported by completed or stored work
explanation: The architect evaluates the application based on observed progress and supporting documentation, then certifies the amount properly due.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: Concrete test reports show compressive strength below specified requirements. What is the best next step?
options: Notify the owner and contractor, require evaluation by the design team, and follow the contract correction process
Issue final payment immediately
Tell the testing agency to change the report
Approve the work and certify payment to avoid any schedule delay
correctOption: Notify the owner and contractor, require evaluation by the design team, and follow the contract correction process
explanation: Failed tests require documented evaluation by the responsible design professionals and appropriate correction or acceptance under the contract.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: The contractor asks for approval to cover waterproofing before inspection. What should the architect consider?
options: Whether the contractor prefers to move faster
Document the deficiency, coordinate technical evaluation, and require retesting or correction as needed
Whether final payment has been made
Whether required observations, tests, and special inspections have been completed before concealment
correctOption: Whether required observations, tests, and special inspections have been completed before concealment
explanation: Work that will be concealed should not be covered before required reviews, tests, or inspections are complete.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: What is the purpose of a field report?
options: Document observations and communicate concerns without directing construction means and methods
To certify code approval
To replace the construction contract
To document observations, progress, issues, weather, attendees, and action items from a site visit
correctOption: To document observations, progress, issues, weather, attendees, and action items from a site visit
explanation: Field reports create a contemporaneous record of site observations and project issues without directing means and methods.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor submits a shop drawing with dimensions that conflict with field conditions. Who is primarily responsible for verifying field dimensions?
options: The building occupant
The contractor
The product manufacturer only
The owner lender
correctOption: The contractor
explanation: The contractor is typically responsible for field measurements, fabrication dimensions, means, methods, and coordination of shop drawings.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A substitution is proposed after award because the specified product has a long lead time. What should be reviewed?
options: Check conformance with contract requirements while preserving contractor responsibility
Accept the proposal if it appears equivalent without supporting data
Compliance, compatibility, schedule impact, cost impact, warranty, and design intent
Use the submittal review as approval of means and methods
correctOption: Compliance, compatibility, schedule impact, cost impact, warranty, and design intent
explanation: Post-award substitutions should be evaluated for technical compliance and project impacts before acceptance.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: The owner wants to occupy one floor before the rest of the building is substantially complete. What should be coordinated?
options: Preserve owner and contractor responsibilities under the agreement
Insurer consent, authority approvals, safety, responsibilities, and partial occupancy documentation
Issue the Certificate of Substantial Completion immediately
Get a verbal approval from the general contractor only
correctOption: Insurer consent, authority approvals, safety, responsibilities, and partial occupancy documentation
explanation: Partial occupancy can affect insurance, life safety, access, warranties, responsibilities, and authority approvals.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: What is the architect role regarding contractor safety programs under a typical AIA contract?
options: Prepare the contractor daily safety plan
Observe and report concerns without controlling means, methods, or safety programs
Operate construction equipment
Train every worker on site
correctOption: Observe and report concerns without controlling means, methods, or safety programs
explanation: The contractor is responsible for construction means, methods, and safety. The architect should not assume control, but should communicate observed concerns appropriately.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor requests additional compensation due to a change directed by the owner. What should be used if the owner and contractor agree on scope, cost, and time?
options: Change Order
Architect Supplemental Instruction
Certificate of insurance
Punch list
correctOption: Change Order
explanation: A change order documents agreed changes to the work, contract sum, or contract time.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: What is retainage?
options: A zoning calculation
A permit review fee
A consultant bonus
A portion of each payment withheld as security for completion of the work
correctOption: A portion of each payment withheld as security for completion of the work
explanation: Retainage provides owner security and incentive for completion, subject to contract terms and release provisions.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor submits an RFI that proposes a cheaper detail with reduced performance. What should the architect do?
options: Tell the owner to inspect it after occupancy
Check conformance with contract requirements while preserving contractor responsibility
Approve it because it saves money
Evaluate the proposal against contract requirements and reject or revise it if performance is reduced
correctOption: Evaluate the proposal against contract requirements and reject or revise it if performance is reduced
explanation: RFI responses should protect design intent, code compliance, and contract requirements. Cost savings do not justify reduced required performance without proper approval.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A concealed condition is discovered that materially differs from the contract documents. What is the contractor required to do under AIA A201?
options: Proceed silently and claim costs at final payment
Provide prompt notice before the condition is disturbed when possible
Destroy evidence to avoid delay
Ask the architect to pay directly
correctOption: Provide prompt notice before the condition is disturbed when possible
explanation: Prompt notice allows the condition to be investigated and any adjustment handled through the contract process.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor fails to maintain required record documents on site. Why does this matter?
options: They replace all inspections
They eliminate warranties
Check conformance with contract requirements while preserving contractor responsibility
Record documents support closeout, owner operations, and future alterations
correctOption: Record documents support closeout, owner operations, and future alterations
explanation: Accurate record documents are important for closeout, maintenance, future renovations, and owner facility management.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: What should the architect do with a submittal marked approved as noted?
options: Confirm that required corrections are incorporated before fabrication or installation as applicable
Assume all future versions are unnecessary
Use it as a change order
Change the contractor means and methods
correctOption: Confirm that required corrections are incorporated before fabrication or installation as applicable
explanation: Approved-as-noted submittals still require the contractor to incorporate comments and remain responsible for coordination and compliance.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor asks for payment for stored custom curtain wall units at an off-site warehouse. What is most important before certification?
options: A verbal promise only
The architect personal inspection of every screw
Review supporting documentation and observed progress before certification
Proof of ownership, insurance, secure storage, identification for the project, and contract compliance
correctOption: Proof of ownership, insurance, secure storage, identification for the project, and contract compliance
explanation: Payment for stored materials requires documentation that materials are properly stored, insured, owned or assigned, and dedicated to the project.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: The owner asks the architect to reject work because the owner dislikes the color, but the color matches approved submittals and specifications. What should the architect do?
options: Review the contract documents and approved submittals before determining whether the work is nonconforming
Approve a change without documenting cost
Order replacement at contractor cost without review
Check conformance with contract requirements while preserving contractor responsibility
correctOption: Review the contract documents and approved submittals before determining whether the work is nonconforming
explanation: Rejection should be based on nonconformance with the contract documents, not preference alone. Desired changes may require a contract change.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A pre-installation conference is most useful for which activity?
options: Selecting the architect contract form
Coordinating responsibilities, substrate conditions, sequencing, testing, and quality expectations before installation
Replacing all submittals
Approving final payment
correctOption: Coordinating responsibilities, substrate conditions, sequencing, testing, and quality expectations before installation
explanation: Pre-installation meetings help prevent field problems by aligning expectations before critical work begins.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: What is substantial completion?
options: The point when the work or a designated portion is sufficiently complete for the owner to use it for its intended purpose
The date the architect signs the owner agreement
The first day of construction
The date bids are opened
correctOption: The point when the work or a designated portion is sufficiently complete for the owner to use it for its intended purpose
explanation: Substantial completion establishes an important milestone for occupancy/use, warranties, insurance, punch list, and retainage.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A punch list item remains incomplete at final payment review. What should the architect do?
options: Certify payment subject to later correction without noting exceptions
Issue the final certificate immediately to preserve the contractor relationship
Tell the owner to fix it
Confirm whether final payment should be withheld or reduced according to the contract until requirements are met
correctOption: Confirm whether final payment should be withheld or reduced according to the contract until requirements are met
explanation: Final payment generally requires completion of remaining work and required closeout documents, subject to contract terms.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: Before final payment, which closeout item is commonly required from the contractor?
options: Review supporting documentation and observed progress before certification
Affidavits, releases or waivers, consent of surety if required, warranties, and record documents
Treat the subcontractor invoice as sufficient proof of entitlement
Certify payment subject to later correction without noting exceptions
correctOption: Affidavits, releases or waivers, consent of surety if required, warranties, and record documents
explanation: Final payment is typically conditioned on completion, closeout documents, releases, warranties, and other contract requirements.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: The contractor claims a delay caused by unusually severe weather. What is the architect most likely evaluating?
options: Whether the owner can revise the substantial completion date without documentation
Whether the contract allows a time extension and whether notice and documentation support the claim
Whether weather data should be ignored if the contractor is already behind schedule
Whether the contractor receives additional compensation for every weather day
correctOption: Whether the contract allows a time extension and whether notice and documentation support the claim
explanation: Weather claims require review of contract provisions, notice, schedule impact, documentation, and whether the delay is excusable or compensable.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A commissioning report identifies that airflows do not meet specified values. What should happen next?
options: The architect should adjust equipment without the engineer
Final payment should be automatic
The issue should be tracked, corrected, retested, and documented before acceptance
Document the deficiency, coordinate technical evaluation, and require retesting or correction as needed
correctOption: The issue should be tracked, corrected, retested, and documented before acceptance
explanation: Commissioning deficiencies should be documented, assigned, corrected, retested, and closed out according to the commissioning plan and contract documents.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor installs an unapproved product that appears equal to the specified product. What is the architect best response?
options: Treat it as a nonconforming or unauthorized substitution until properly reviewed
Tell the owner it cannot be reviewed
Pay extra for it immediately
Approve the product since it appears visually equivalent
correctOption: Treat it as a nonconforming or unauthorized substitution until properly reviewed
explanation: Products should comply with the contract documents and approved submittals. Unauthorized substitutions require review and may be rejected.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: The owner wants to terminate the contractor for convenience. What should the architect remember?
options: Termination rights and payment obligations depend on the owner-contractor agreement and general conditions
The architect can terminate the contractor alone
Convenience termination has no cost consequences
Follow the contract communication process and document the observed condition
correctOption: Termination rights and payment obligations depend on the owner-contractor agreement and general conditions
explanation: Termination for convenience is a contractual remedy with notice and payment consequences. The architect should avoid giving legal advice beyond contract administration duties.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A special inspector reports failed masonry prism tests. What should the architect do?
options: Approve final payment immediately
Issue a certificate of occupancy
Coordinate review by the structural engineer and require correction or evaluation under the contract
Tell the mason to ignore the tests
correctOption: Coordinate review by the structural engineer and require correction or evaluation under the contract
explanation: Failed special inspections or tests require technical evaluation and documented resolution before affected work is accepted.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: Who is typically responsible for construction means, methods, techniques, sequences, and procedures?
options: The architect
The contractor
The building occupant
The lender
correctOption: The contractor
explanation: Under typical AIA general conditions, the contractor controls construction means, methods, sequences, procedures, and safety.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor asks whether an ASI can be used for a change that increases contract sum. What is the correct response?
options: Yes, if the contractor agrees to track the cost after the work is complete
Yes, if the change is shown on an architect-issued sketch
No. An ASI is for minor changes or clarifications that do not affect cost or time
No, because ASIs are used solely for substitutions during procurement
correctOption: No. An ASI is for minor changes or clarifications that do not affect cost or time
explanation: Architect supplemental instructions should not change contract sum or contract time. Cost/time changes require the proper change process.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor submits a schedule recovery plan after falling behind. What should the architect review?
options: Whether the plan reduces code-required inspections
Whether the recovery plan reduces required inspections to gain time
Whether the plan extends final completion without a formal change order
Whether the plan reasonably addresses sequencing, milestones, resources, and impacts without reducing contract requirements
correctOption: Whether the plan reasonably addresses sequencing, milestones, resources, and impacts without reducing contract requirements
explanation: Recovery plans should address schedule performance while maintaining quality, safety, inspections, and contract requirements.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor requests liquidated damages be waived because the owner suffered no easily measurable actual loss. What is the key contract concept?
options: Liquidated damages can be added after bidding without agreement by the contractor
Liquidated damages are a substitute for documenting the contract completion date
Liquidated damages may be enforceable if they were a reasonable forecast of damages and not a penalty
Liquidated damages become unenforceable whenever actual damages are difficult to calculate
correctOption: Liquidated damages may be enforceable if they were a reasonable forecast of damages and not a penalty
explanation: Liquidated damages are agreed amounts for delay damages; enforceability depends on contract language and whether they are punitive.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: The owner reports a defect discovered after final completion but within the correction period. What should the architect do?
options: Tell the owner no correction is possible after final payment
Repair it personally
Notify the contractor and administer the correction process according to the contract
Issue a zoning variance
correctOption: Notify the contractor and administer the correction process according to the contract
explanation: Correction obligations may continue after substantial or final completion depending on the contract and warranties.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor requests a change order for additional work performed without authorization. What should the architect evaluate?
options: Whether the contractor can show labor and material costs after performing the work
Whether the owner verbally mentioned similar work in an earlier meeting
Whether the work was authorized by the contract documents or proper change process before recommending payment
Whether the added work improves the project even without authorization
correctOption: Whether the work was authorized by the contract documents or proper change process before recommending payment
explanation: Additional payment generally requires contractual authorization or entitlement. Unauthorized work is not automatically compensable.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A claim is submitted after the contract notice period has expired. What is the architect best action?
options: Review the contract notice requirements and process the claim according to the agreement without making informal promises
Approve the claim if the underlying condition appears legitimate
Reject the claim by field report without reviewing the notice provisions
Advise the contractor to bypass mediation and proceed directly to litigation
correctOption: Review the contract notice requirements and process the claim according to the agreement without making informal promises
explanation: Claims must be handled under the contract requirements for notice, timing, documentation, and initial decision procedures.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor damages existing work while performing renovation work. Who typically bears responsibility for correcting the damage?
options: The contractor, unless the contract states otherwise or the damage was caused by owner direction
The building official
The architect, because the architect approved the contractor's work method
The future tenant
correctOption: The contractor, unless the contract states otherwise or the damage was caused by owner direction
explanation: The contractor is typically responsible for protecting work and correcting damage caused by its operations.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: What is a certificate for payment?
options: A zoning approval
A building permit
The architect certification of the amount believed due to the contractor based on the payment application and observed progress
A shop drawing
correctOption: The architect certification of the amount believed due to the contractor based on the payment application and observed progress
explanation: A certificate for payment communicates the architect judgment of the amount due under the contract, subject to contract limitations.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A field report notes that work is behind schedule. What should it avoid?
options: Identifying action items
Documenting observed progress
Listing weather conditions
Directing the contractor means, methods, or sequencing unless the contract allows it
correctOption: Directing the contractor means, methods, or sequencing unless the contract allows it
explanation: The architect can document progress and concerns but should avoid taking control of contractor means, methods, sequences, or safety.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: An owner wants to accept nonconforming work in exchange for a credit. What should be documented?
options: A field report noting the credit but no owner acceptance
Owner acceptance, design-team evaluation, code compliance, and a change order or appropriate contract modification
A pay application reduction without evaluating code or performance impacts
A contractor letter stating the condition will remain concealed
correctOption: Owner acceptance, design-team evaluation, code compliance, and a change order or appropriate contract modification
explanation: Accepting nonconforming work can affect performance, code, warranty, and value. It should be evaluated and documented formally.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: What is the purpose of O&M manuals at closeout?
options: To establish zoning use
To help the owner operate and maintain building systems and products
To replace construction drawings
To approve the contractor bid
correctOption: To help the owner operate and maintain building systems and products
explanation: Operation and maintenance manuals support owner training, maintenance, warranty management, and facility operations.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A required permit inspection fails. What should happen before the work proceeds?
options: The report should be ignored
The deficiency should be corrected and re-inspected or otherwise resolved with the authority having jurisdiction
The contractor should conceal the work
The architect should issue final payment
correctOption: The deficiency should be corrected and re-inspected or otherwise resolved with the authority having jurisdiction
explanation: Failed authority inspections must be resolved before affected work proceeds or is accepted.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: A contractor requests release of retainage at substantial completion while punch list work remains. What should the architect review?
options: Contract provisions for retainage reduction, value of incomplete work, owner occupancy, and remaining obligations
Review supporting documentation and observed progress before certification
Certify payment subject to later correction without noting exceptions
Treat the subcontractor invoice as sufficient proof of entitlement
correctOption: Contract provisions for retainage reduction, value of incomplete work, owner occupancy, and remaining obligations
explanation: Retainage release at substantial completion depends on contract terms and the value and risk of remaining work and closeout obligations.
codeReference: NCARB ARE 5.0 Guidelines, April 2026 - Construction & Evaluation; AIA A201-2017

question: Under AIA A201, what is the architect's role during construction site visits?
options: To supervise all subcontractor work daily
To control site safety
To become generally familiar with work progress and quality through periodic observations, not continuous inspection
To direct the contractor's means and methods
correctOption: To become generally familiar with work progress and quality through periodic observations, not continuous inspection
explanation: AIA B101 Section 3.6 and A201 Section 4.2.2 describe the architect's construction phase role as periodic observation—not continuous supervision. The contractor controls means, methods, and site safety. The architect confirms general conformance with contract documents.
codeReference: AIA B101-2017 Section 3.6.2; AIA A201-2017 Section 4.2.2

question: What is a submittal in construction administration?
options: A contractor-provided document (shop drawing, sample, product data) submitted for the architect's review to confirm conformance with contract documents
The contractor's formal bid document
A certificate of insurance from the contractor
A government permit application
correctOption: A contractor-provided document (shop drawing, sample, product data) submitted for the architect's review to confirm conformance with contract documents
explanation: Submittals include shop drawings, samples, product data, and calculations that the contractor prepares or obtains from suppliers. The architect reviews them to confirm they conform to the design intent in the contract documents—not to take on fabrication responsibility.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 3.12; NCARB CE Objective 2.2

question: What document does the architect issue to provide clarifications or minor changes that do not change the contract sum or time?
options: Certificate of Substantial Completion (G704)
Construction Change Directive (G714)
Change Order (G701)
Architect's Supplemental Instructions (ASI) – AIA G710
correctOption: Architect's Supplemental Instructions (ASI) – AIA G710
explanation: An ASI (AIA G710) is used for clarifications and minor changes that do not affect the contract sum or contract time. Changes that affect cost or time require a Change Order (G701) signed by the owner, contractor, and architect.
codeReference: AIA G710-1992; AIA A201-2017 Section 7.4

question: What is the purpose of a pre-bid conference?
options: To negotiate the contract price with the lowest bidder
To allow all bidders to ask questions about the project and for the architect to provide consistent clarifications to all parties
To conduct a site safety orientation for workers
To review shop drawings submitted before construction begins
correctOption: To allow all bidders to ask questions about the project and for the architect to provide consistent clarifications to all parties
explanation: A pre-bid conference gives all interested bidders equal access to information and allows them to ask questions. Answers are distributed to all bidders through an addendum, ensuring a fair and competitive bidding process.
codeReference: AIA A701-2018; NCARB CE Objective 1.1

question: What is a punch list?
options: A list of required permits
The contractor's daily safety inspection report
A list of items the contractor must complete or correct before final completion and final payment
A list of subcontractors on the project
correctOption: A list of items the contractor must complete or correct before final completion and final payment
explanation: After the architect determines the project has reached substantial completion, the architect and contractor jointly develop a punch list of remaining incomplete or defective items. The contractor completes punch list items to achieve final completion and receive final payment.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 9.8.2; NCARB CE Objective 3.1

question: Which AIA document does a contractor use to request payment for work completed during a construction period?
options: AIA G704 – Certificate of Substantial Completion
AIA G701 – Change Order
AIA G710 – Architect's Supplemental Instructions
AIA G702 – Application and Certificate for Payment
correctOption: AIA G702 – Application and Certificate for Payment
explanation: AIA G702 is the Application and Certificate for Payment. The contractor submits it monthly with the G703 continuation sheet (schedule of values breakdown). The architect reviews and certifies the amount due, then the owner pays.
codeReference: AIA G702-1992; AIA A201-2017 Section 9.4

question: What is a Construction Change Directive (CCD)?
options: A directive signed by the owner and architect requiring the contractor to proceed with a change before price and time adjustments are agreed
A subcontractor's notice of a delay
A clarification that doesn't affect cost
A change order signed by all parties
correctOption: A directive signed by the owner and architect requiring the contractor to proceed with a change before price and time adjustments are agreed
explanation: AIA G714 (Construction Change Directive) must be signed by the owner and architect. AIA A201-2017 §7.3 authorizes this joint directive when an immediate change is needed but the parties cannot reach agreement on price or time. The contractor must proceed; cost and schedule impacts are resolved later by negotiation or through the claims process. A CCD is NOT a unilateral owner action — the architect's signature is required.
codeReference: AIA G714-2017; AIA A201-2017 Section 7.3

question: What is the 'Initial Decision Maker' (IDM) role under AIA A201?
options: The architect, who makes the initial decision on claims and disputes between the owner and contractor before other dispute resolution methods are invoked
The owner's attorney
A neutral third-party arbitrator
The general contractor's project manager
correctOption: The architect, who makes the initial decision on claims and disputes between the owner and contractor before other dispute resolution methods are invoked
explanation: Under AIA A201 Section 15.2, the architect serves as the Initial Decision Maker (IDM). The architect reviews claims, requests supplemental information, and issues an Initial Decision. Either party may then request mediation or arbitration if dissatisfied.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 15.2; NCARB CE Objective 4.1

question: What happens to the contractor's retainage at the time of substantial completion?
options: It is transferred to the architect as a holdback fee
It is typically reduced or fully released (per the contract), with the remaining balance paid after final completion
It doubles to cover the punch list
It is forfeited to the owner
correctOption: It is typically reduced or fully released (per the contract), with the remaining balance paid after final completion
explanation: At substantial completion, many contracts reduce retainage from 10% to 5% (or release it entirely for completed portions), with the balance held until final completion. This rewards the contractor for reaching beneficial occupancy while still ensuring punch list completion.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 9.8.5; NCARB CE Objective 3.1

question: What is the primary purpose of a performance bond in a construction contract?
options: To guarantee the contractor will complete the project according to the contract terms
To guarantee payment of subcontractors
To cover the architect's professional liability
To insure the owner's property
correctOption: To guarantee the contractor will complete the project according to the contract terms
explanation: A performance bond is a surety instrument guaranteeing the contractor's performance. If the contractor defaults, the surety (bond company) is obligated to complete the project or pay the owner up to the bond amount. A separate payment bond guarantees payment to subcontractors and suppliers.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 11.6; NCARB CE Objective 1.2

question: Which document establishes the date that contractor warranties begin?
options: Certificate of Substantial Completion (AIA G704)
Building permit
Notice to Proceed
Certificate of Final Completion
correctOption: Certificate of Substantial Completion (AIA G704)
explanation: Under AIA A201 Section 9.8.5, the Certificate of Substantial Completion (G704) establishes the date from which contractor and manufacturer warranties begin. This protects the owner by starting warranty periods when they can actually use and observe the completed building.
codeReference: AIA G704-2017; AIA A201-2017 Section 9.8.5

question: When the architect makes a site observation and sees defective work, what is the FIRST appropriate action?
options: Document it in writing and notify the contractor to correct the work
Immediately issue a stop-work order to the entire project
Correct the work directly
Tell the owner to fire the contractor
correctOption: Document it in writing and notify the contractor to correct the work
explanation: The architect documents observations in writing (site observation report or field report) and notifies the contractor of non-conforming work. The contractor is responsible for correcting defective work per AIA A201 Section 12.2. A stop-work order is reserved for serious safety threats or owner direction.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 12.2; AIA B101-2017 Section 3.6.3

question: What is the purpose of the liquidated damages clause in a construction contract?
options: To set the fee for change orders
To establish a pre-agreed per-day dollar amount the contractor owes the owner for each day of unexcused delay beyond the contract completion date
To penalize the architect for design errors
To calculate the contractor's profit
correctOption: To establish a pre-agreed per-day dollar amount the contractor owes the owner for each day of unexcused delay beyond the contract completion date
explanation: Liquidated damages (LD) provisions avoid litigation over delay damages by pre-establishing a reasonable daily rate. They represent an estimate of the owner's actual damages from delay (lost revenue, additional rental costs) and are enforceable when they are not a penalty.
codeReference: AIA A101-2017 Section 3.3; AIA A201-2017 Section 8.3; NCARB CE Objective 4.1

question: During bidding, which action is most appropriate when bids exceed the owner's budget?
options: Evaluate alternates, scope, unit prices, substitutions, and rebidding options with documented impacts
Delete code-required work
Ignore the overage
Tell the contractor to lower the price without scope changes
correctOption: Evaluate alternates, scope, unit prices, substitutions, and rebidding options with documented impacts
explanation: Bid overages should be resolved through documented options that preserve code compliance and owner priorities.
codeReference: AIA A701-2018; NCARB CE Objective 1.3

question: At a preconstruction conference, what is the architect's role when reviewing shop drawings?
options: Guarantee dimensions field measured by subcontractors
Approve contractor means and methods
Redesign the contractor's work without compensation
Review for general conformance with design intent and contract documents, not means and methods
correctOption: Review for general conformance with design intent and contract documents, not means and methods
explanation: Submittal review is limited and does not transfer contractor responsibility for means, methods, or field dimensions.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 4.2.7; NCARB CE Objective 3.2

question: When reviewing a submittal, which response is best after observing work that does not conform to the contract documents?
options: Direct subcontractors to stop all work permanently
Ignore the issue until closeout
Document the observation and notify the owner and contractor consistent with contract procedures
Approve payment for the nonconforming work without comment
correctOption: Document the observation and notify the owner and contractor consistent with contract procedures
explanation: The architect reports observed nonconformance through the contract communication structure and may reject work as allowed by the contract.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Sections 4.2 and 12.2; NCARB CE Objective 3.4

question: After observing nonconforming work on site, which information should be checked before certifying a payment application?
options: Only the weather that week
Only the architect's design fee
Only the contractor's logo
Work completed, stored materials, retainage, previous payments, change orders, and observed progress
correctOption: Work completed, stored materials, retainage, previous payments, change orders, and observed progress
explanation: Payment certification requires evaluating the contractor's application against progress and contract requirements.
codeReference: AIA G702-1992; AIA A201-2017 Section 9.4; NCARB CE Objective 3.3

question: When evaluating a payment application, which document is typically used to modify contract sum or time after agreement by owner and contractor?
options: Submittal log
Change order
Meeting agenda
RFI
correctOption: Change order
explanation: A change order records an agreed modification to contract sum, contract time, or scope after the owner and contractor accept the change; it is not merely a question or submittal.
codeReference: AIA G701-2017; AIA A201-2017 Article 7; NCARB CE Objective 3.1

question: After a contractor submits an RFI, which item is most appropriate for an RFI?
options: A payment certificate
A request for clarification of the contract documents where information is missing or ambiguous
A substitute for shop drawing review
A unilateral change order
correctOption: A request for clarification of the contract documents where information is missing or ambiguous
explanation: RFIs clarify documents but should not be used to shift design responsibility or authorize changes without proper procedure.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 4.2.14; NCARB CE Objective 3.1

question: When the owner asks for a change during construction, which action should the architect take when a submittal proposes a substitution?
options: Let the subcontractor decide
Reject without review regardless of requirements
Follow substitution procedures and evaluate whether the proposed product meets specified criteria
Approve because it is cheaper
correctOption: Follow substitution procedures and evaluate whether the proposed product meets specified criteria
explanation: Substitutions should be reviewed under contract procedures against performance, design intent, and documentation requirements.
codeReference: CSI substitution procedures; AIA A201-2017 Section 3.4.2; NCARB CE Objective 3.2

question: Before issuing a certificate of substantial completion, which activity is part of substantial completion review?
options: Accept responsibility for site safety
Determine whether the work is sufficiently complete for the owner to occupy or use it for its intended purpose
Release all retainage automatically
Guarantee all work is perfect
correctOption: Determine whether the work is sufficiently complete for the owner to occupy or use it for its intended purpose
explanation: Substantial completion is about usability for intended purpose, with remaining punch-list work documented.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 9.8; AIA G704-2017; NCARB CE Objective 4.1

question: During the bidding phase, which activity ensures all bidders receive consistent clarifications about project requirements before submitting bids?
options: Final certificate of payment
Post-occupancy survey
Warranty inspection
Pre-bid conference and documented addenda for clarifications
correctOption: Pre-bid conference and documented addenda for clarifications
explanation: Pre-bid conferences and addenda communicate clarifications consistently to bidders.
codeReference: AIA A701-2018; NCARB CE Objective 1.1

question: During contractor prequalification for a competitively bid project, which criterion is most important when evaluating bidder qualifications?
options: Office logo color
Lowest marketing expense
Relevant experience, capacity, financial stability, safety record, references, and schedule capability
Architect's preferred lunch location
correctOption: Relevant experience, capacity, financial stability, safety record, references, and schedule capability
explanation: Qualification review should weigh capability, relevant experience, capacity, safety, references, and risk so the owner can judge whether the contractor can actually deliver the work.
codeReference: AIA A701-2018; NCARB CE Objective 1.2

question: During bidding, which observation best fits the architect's construction phase services?
options: Periodic site visits to become generally familiar with progress and quality of the work
Continuous inspection of every activity
Direction of subcontractor means and methods
Control of jobsite safety
correctOption: Periodic site visits to become generally familiar with progress and quality of the work
explanation: Architect observation is periodic and limited unless expanded by contract; it does not make the architect the constructor.
codeReference: AIA B101-2017; AIA A201-2017 Section 4.2.2; NCARB CE Objective 2.1

question: During a construction site visit, which items should the architect document in an observation field report?
options: Only lunch orders
Observed progress, deviations, weather/context, attendees, decisions, and items requiring action
Only personal opinions unrelated to work
Only private consultant salary data
correctOption: Observed progress, deviations, weather/context, attendees, decisions, and items requiring action
explanation: Field reports create a contemporaneous record of observations, progress, and action items.
codeReference: CSI Construction Contract Administration; NCARB CE Objective 2.3

question: When reviewing a submittal, which action is best when test results show concrete strength below specified requirements?
options: Ask the architect to redesign secretly
Approve all related work
Ignore if the surface looks acceptable
Notify appropriate parties and require evaluation by the design team/testing agency under contract procedures
correctOption: Notify appropriate parties and require evaluation by the design team/testing agency under contract procedures
explanation: Failed tests require formal evaluation and corrective action, not informal acceptance.
codeReference: ACI 318; IBC 2021 Chapter 17; AIA A201-2017; NCARB CE Objective 2.2

question: After observing nonconforming work on site, which closeout item confirms required maintenance and operation information is turned over?
options: Schematic design narrative
Pre-bid agenda
Bid tabulation
Operations and maintenance manuals
correctOption: Operations and maintenance manuals
explanation: O&M manuals support owner operation, maintenance, and warranty management after occupancy.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 9.10; CSI closeout procedures; NCARB CE Objective 4.1

question: When evaluating a payment application, which response is best when the owner wants to occupy before all punch-list work is complete?
options: Tell contractor to ignore punch-list work
Declare final completion immediately
Evaluate substantial completion, life-safety readiness, AHJ approvals, insurance, warranties, and responsibilities
Allow occupancy with no documentation
correctOption: Evaluate substantial completion, life-safety readiness, AHJ approvals, insurance, warranties, and responsibilities
explanation: Early occupancy requires careful documentation of readiness, responsibilities, insurance, and remaining work.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Sections 9.8 and 9.9; NCARB CE Objective 4.1

question: After a contractor submits an RFI, which submittal is most likely used to verify appearance and workmanship before full installation?
options: Lien waiver
RFI log
Mock-up
Payment application
correctOption: Mock-up
explanation: Mock-ups allow review of visual quality, workmanship, interfaces, and performance before repetitive work proceeds.
codeReference: CSI submittal procedures; NCARB CE Objective 3.2

question: When the owner asks for a change during construction, which party is generally responsible for construction means, methods, techniques, sequences, and safety?
options: Building occupants
Owner's lender
Contractor
Architect
correctOption: Contractor
explanation: Under standard AIA conditions, the contractor controls construction means and methods and site safety.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 3.3; NCARB CE Objective 2.1

question: Before issuing a certificate of substantial completion, which action best handles concealed conditions discovered during construction?
options: Require contractor to absorb all costs automatically
Proceed without documentation
Ignore until final completion
Follow contract notice procedures, document conditions, and evaluate cost/time impact
correctOption: Follow contract notice procedures, document conditions, and evaluate cost/time impact
explanation: Concealed conditions require timely notice and evaluation under the contract's change procedures.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 3.7.4 and Article 7; NCARB CE Objective 3.1

question: During closeout, which item is most important when reviewing a contractor's proposed construction schedule?
options: Sequencing, milestones, long-lead items, critical path, owner constraints, and coordination requirements
Contractor's office location only
Color of schedule bars only
Number of pages only
correctOption: Sequencing, milestones, long-lead items, critical path, owner constraints, and coordination requirements
explanation: Schedule review focuses on whether sequencing and milestones support contract requirements and coordination.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 3.10; NCARB CE Objective 2.3

question: After occupancy begins, which response is most appropriate when the contractor asks for approval of a product that does not meet the specified fire rating?
options: Approve if cheaper
Ignore the rating
Reject or require compliant resubmittal unless a properly approved contract change revises the requirement
Approve because it is available
correctOption: Reject or require compliant resubmittal unless a properly approved contract change revises the requirement
explanation: Life-safety performance requirements cannot be waived informally through submittal review.
codeReference: IBC 2021 Chapter 7; AIA A201-2017; NCARB CE Objective 3.2

question: After a building has been occupied for approximately one year, which activity best evaluates whether the project meets user needs and performs as designed?
options: Pre-bid conference only
Submittal review only
Bid opening
Post-occupancy evaluation using occupant feedback, performance data, and operational observations
correctOption: Post-occupancy evaluation using occupant feedback, performance data, and operational observations
explanation: Post-occupancy evaluation compares actual performance and user experience to project goals.
codeReference: NCARB CE Objective 4.2

question: At a preconstruction conference, which action is best when an RFI response changes contract cost or time?
options: Process the cost/time impact through the contract change procedure
Ignore the impact
Treat the RFI response as free work
Revise only the architect's file copy
correctOption: Process the cost/time impact through the contract change procedure
explanation: RFIs clarify information; cost or time changes require formal change procedures.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Article 7; NCARB CE Objective 3.1

question: When reviewing a submittal, which document is generally issued to all bidders to modify bidding documents before bids are due?
options: Change order
Punch list
Addendum
Certificate of payment
correctOption: Addendum
explanation: Addenda modify or clarify bidding documents before bids are submitted and should be distributed consistently.
codeReference: AIA A701-2018 Section 3.3; NCARB CE Objective 1.1

question: The contractor has submitted a final payment request. Which conditions must be satisfied before the architect can certify final completion?
options: Completion of punch-list items, closeout submittals, final inspections, and required releases
A kickoff agenda
A preliminary rendering
A concept estimate
correctOption: Completion of punch-list items, closeout submittals, final inspections, and required releases
explanation: Final completion requires completion of remaining work and closeout obligations under the contract.
codeReference: AIA A201-2017 Section 9.10; NCARB CE Objective 4.1

question: When evaluating a payment application, which action best addresses recurring water leakage reported after occupancy?
options: Issue a change order without investigation
Assume occupant misuse without review
Delete the issue from records
Review warranty status, observe conditions, document symptoms, and evaluate design/construction/maintenance causes
correctOption: Review warranty status, observe conditions, document symptoms, and evaluate design/construction/maintenance causes
explanation: Post-occupancy performance issues require documented evaluation of potential causes and responsibilities.
codeReference: NCARB CE Objective 4.2; building enclosure commissioning principles

question: Under the current NYC Zoning Resolution, what is the standard residential FAR for an R6 zoning lot that is not qualifying for affordable or senior housing floor area?
explanation: After the December 5, 2024 zoning text update, NYC Zoning Resolution Section 23-22 lists R6 standard residences at 2.20 FAR and qualifying affordable or senior housing at 3.60 FAR. Older height-factor references such as 2.43 should not be used for current zoning analysis without confirming vesting or legacy applicability.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Section 23-22, last amended Dec. 5, 2024

question: A NYC residential project seeks additional floor area by providing qualifying affordable housing under the post-2024 zoning text. Which current zoning framework should the architect check first?
options: A sky exposure plane exception unrelated to affordable housing
A zoning lot merger that automatically waives FAR limits
A BSA variance for any project with affordable units
Universal Affordability Preference or Mandatory Inclusionary Housing provisions
correctOption: Universal Affordability Preference or Mandatory Inclusionary Housing provisions
explanation: The current NYC Zoning Resolution uses Article II, Chapter 7 affordable-housing administration with UAP and MIH terminology, while some pre-2024 Inclusionary Housing provisions remain relevant only for vested projects. The architect should confirm the applicable current zoning framework rather than assuming an older Inclusionary Housing bonus applies.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Article II, Chapter 7; NYC HPD Inclusionary Housing / UAP guidance

question: Under the NYC Zoning Resolution, a legally non-conforming use may continue, but it cannot be:
options: Operated by a new tenant
Repaired after minor damage
Transferred with the property upon sale
Extended or expanded into a new part of the building
correctOption: Extended or expanded into a new part of the building
explanation: NYC ZR Section 52-00 allows non-conforming uses to continue and be transferred with the property, but they cannot be extended or expanded. If the non-conforming use is discontinued for two years, it may not resume.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution S.52-00 et seq.

question: In NYC, how does a BSA special permit differ from a zoning variance?
options: A special permit is the same as an appeal from a DOB Building Code objection
A special permit requires the same unnecessary-hardship finding as a use variance
A special permit may be granted for specific uses or modifications listed in the Zoning Resolution when required findings are met
A special permit changes the zoning map district for the parcel
correctOption: A special permit may be granted for specific uses or modifications listed in the Zoning Resolution when required findings are met
explanation: A BSA special permit is a discretionary approval available only where the Zoning Resolution authorizes it and where required findings are satisfied. A variance is different because it is relief from strict zoning compliance based on hardship or practical difficulty standards.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution S.73-00; NYC Charter S.668

question: Under the 2022 NYC Building Code, a new high-rise residential building (over 75 ft in height) must be equipped with an automatic sprinkler system throughout. What is the primary code section governing this requirement?
options: BC Section 903.2.8
BC Section 907.2.9
BC Section 915.1
BC Section 903.3.1.1
correctOption: BC Section 903.2.8
explanation: 2022 NYC Building Code Section 903.2.8 requires automatic sprinkler systems in Group R occupancies in buildings over 75 feet in height. Section 903.3.1.1 governs the NFPA 13 standard for the sprinkler system design itself.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code S.903.2.8

question: The 2022 NYC Building Code requires a minimum egress stair width of how many inches for an occupant load of 200 or more?
options: 44 inches
36 inches
56 inches
48 inches
correctOption: 44 inches
explanation: NYC BC Section 1005.1 and Table 1005.1 set the minimum stair width for an occupant load of 50 or more at 44 inches. This is consistent with IBC requirements and ensures adequate egress capacity for larger occupant loads.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code S.1005.1, Table 1005.1

question: Under the 2022 NYC Building Code, which of the following work items triggers a requirement for Special Inspection?
options: High-strength bolting of a structural steel connection
Installation of standard wood framing in a 2-story building
Identify required inspections, responsible agencies, and filing obligations before work begins
Installation of residential vinyl windows
correctOption: High-strength bolting of a structural steel connection
explanation: NYC BC Section 1705.2 requires Special Inspection for structural steel, including high-strength bolting. Special Inspections are mandated for work that is critical to structural integrity and cannot be adequately inspected after completion.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code S.1705.2

question: A NYC Department of Buildings permit issued for new building construction expires if substantial work is not commenced within:
options: 3 years
2 years
6 months
1 year
correctOption: 1 year
explanation: NYC BC Section 105.4.1 states that a work permit expires if the authorized work is not commenced within one year after issuance, or if the work is suspended or abandoned for a period of one year.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code S.105.4.1

question: NYC Local Law 97 of 2019 sets carbon emission caps for buildings over 25,000 sq ft. Non-compliant building owners face a penalty of:
options: $1,000 per day of violation
Mandatory retroactive retrofit to Passive House standard
$268 per metric ton of CO2e over the limit per year
$500 per square foot of the building
correctOption: $268 per metric ton of CO2e over the limit per year
explanation: NYC Local Law 97 (2019) imposes annual penalties of $268 per metric ton of CO2 equivalent that exceeds a building's annual limit. Stricter caps take effect in 2030. The law covers buildings over 25,000 gross sq ft.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 97 of 2019 (NYC Admin. Code S.28-320)

question: NYC Local Law 97 carbon caps apply in two compliance periods. The first period runs from 2024-2029, and the second (stricter) period begins in:
explanation: NYC Local Law 97 establishes two main compliance periods: 2024-2029 (first caps) and 2030-2034 (stricter caps). Buildings must comply annually and report emissions. The goal is an 80% reduction in emissions by 2050.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 97 of 2019 S.28-320.3.2

question: Under NYC Local Law 11 (FISP), a facade inspection report classified as SWARMP means:
options: Safe Without Any Required Maintenance Program
Structurally Weak And Requires Major Patching
Scheduled Work And Recommended Maintenance Program
Safe With A Repair and Maintenance Program
correctOption: Safe With A Repair and Maintenance Program
explanation: The NYC Facade Inspection Safety Program (FISP, formerly Local Law 11) requires inspection of facades on buildings 6+ stories every 5 years. SWARMP (Safe With A Repair and Maintenance Program) means the facade is currently safe but requires repair within the next cycle. UNSAFE facades require immediate remediation and sidewalk shed.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 11 (1998); NYC Admin. Code S.28-302

question: How often must covered NYC buildings generally file a Facade Inspection and Safety Program (FISP) report?
options: Every year
Every 3 years
Every 5 years
At each tenant turnover
correctOption: Every 5 years
explanation: NYC FISP requires owners of covered buildings to have exterior walls and appurtenances inspected and to file a technical report with DOB every five years. A separate question in this bank covers the height threshold for applicability.
codeReference: NYC Admin. Code S.28-302.1

question: NYC Local Law 26 of 2004 required existing office buildings 100 feet or more in height to install full automatic sprinkler systems by a specific deadline. That deadline was:
options: December 31, 2010
December 31, 2013
January 1, 2023
July 1, 2019
correctOption: July 1, 2019
explanation: NYC Local Law 26 of 2004 created a retroactive sprinkler requirement for existing office buildings 100 feet or more in height. Covered owners were required to complete installation and file final compliance documentation by July 1, 2019, subject to limited hardship or landmark-related relief.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 26 of 2004; NYC Administrative Code former Section 27-929.1; NYC DOB Buildings News June 2018

question: An existing NYC office building is 120 feet tall and was in existence when Local Law 26 of 2004 was enacted. What was the primary retroactive sprinkler requirement?
options: Check the applicable NYC code, local law, agency rule, and filing procedure
