The building had to be fully sprinklered by the Local Law 26 compliance deadline, unless limited relief applied
Confirm whether DOB, LPC, BSA, or another agency has jurisdiction
No sprinklers were required if the building had an existing wet standpipe
correctOption: The building had to be fully sprinklered by the Local Law 26 compliance deadline, unless limited relief applied
explanation: Local Law 26 targeted existing office buildings 100 feet or more in height and required full automatic sprinkler protection by the final compliance deadline. Existing standpipes did not, by themselves, satisfy the retroactive sprinkler requirement.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 26 of 2004; NYC Administrative Code former Section 27-929.1; NYC DOB Buildings News June 2018

question: To professionally certify a NYC Department of Buildings permit application, a registered architect must attest that:
options: The contractor has adequate bonding and insurance
The work complies with all applicable laws and the architect takes responsibility for plan examination
The owner has received a Certificate of Occupancy
The building has passed all required inspections
correctOption: The work complies with all applicable laws and the architect takes responsibility for plan examination
explanation: NYC DOB Professional Certification (ProfCert) allows registered architects and engineers to certify that construction documents comply with all applicable laws, bypassing standard DOB plan examination. The design professional assumes responsibility for code compliance. False certification is a Class A misdemeanor.
codeReference: NYC Admin. Code S.28-104.7.12; 1 RCNY S.21-02

question: An architect professionally certifies a DOB permit application in NYC. The DOB later identifies code violations in the approved drawings. Which statement is most accurate?
options: The contractor becomes solely responsible for all design-code violations after permit issuance
DOB cannot review a professionally certified application after approval
The violation is automatically cured if construction has already started
DOB may audit the filing and impose objections, penalties, ProfCert restrictions, or disciplinary referral depending on the violation
correctOption: DOB may audit the filing and impose objections, penalties, ProfCert restrictions, or disciplinary referral depending on the violation
explanation: Professional certification does not eliminate DOB audit authority or the registered design professional responsibility for code compliance. Consequences depend on the nature of the violation and can include objections, civil penalties, exclusion from professional certification privileges, or referral for professional discipline.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code Section 28-104; 1 RCNY Section 101-03; NYC DOB Professional Certification Program

question: An architect in NYC is designing an alteration to a landmarked building. The design must be reviewed and approved by:
options: NYC Department of City Planning (DCP)
NYC Department of Buildings (DOB) only
NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC)
NYC Board of Standards and Appeals (BSA)
correctOption: NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC)
explanation: Any alteration to a NYC landmark, interior landmark, or building within a Historic District requires a Certificate of Appropriateness (or Certificate of No Effect) from the Landmarks Preservation Commission before DOB permits are issued. The LPC reviews proposed work for compatibility with the landmark's character.
codeReference: NYC Admin. Code S.25-306; Landmarks Preservation Commission Rules

question: A developer wants to combine two adjacent NYC tax lots to maximize building area. This is accomplished through a:
options: Zoning lot merger
Special Permit
Variance
Subdivision map amendment
correctOption: Zoning lot merger
explanation: A zoning lot merger under NYC ZR Section 12-10 combines two or more tax lots into a single zoning lot, allowing the total available floor area to be distributed across the combined site. This is commonly used to transfer unused air rights from a low-rise building to an adjacent site.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution S.12-10 (definition of zoning lot)

question: Under the current NYC Zoning Resolution, how should an architect begin calculating permitted floor area for a mixed building with ground-floor retail and residential floors above in a C1-2 commercial overlay?
options: The ground-floor commercial area is excluded from zoning floor area
Apply the C1-2 commercial FAR to the entire mixed building
Use community facility FAR because all mixed buildings are treated as community facility uses
Apply the Article 35 mixed-building rules, using the underlying residence district and applicable commercial overlay limits
correctOption: Apply the Article 35 mixed-building rules, using the underlying residence district and applicable commercial overlay limits
explanation: For C1-1 through C1-5 and C2-1 through C2-5 commercial overlays, permitted commercial, residential, and mixed-building floor area depends on the Residence District in which the overlay is mapped. Article 35 controls mixed buildings, while Article 23 and Article 33 provide the relevant residential and commercial bulk inputs.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Sections 33-121, 35-30, and 23-22

question: Under the NYC Building Code 2022 height and area tables, what is the base maximum building height for a nonsprinklered Type II-B Group B building before any allowable increases or special provisions?
options: No height limit for Type II-B business occupancy
75 feet
65 feet
55 feet
correctOption: 55 feet
explanation: The base height table value for Type II-B construction with Group B occupancy is 55 feet before applying sprinkler-related increases or other special provisions. The question is intentionally asking for the base nonsprinklered table value.
codeReference: NYC Building Code 2022 Table 504.3

question: Which NYC agency is responsible for enforcing the NYC Zoning Resolution?
options: NYC Department of City Planning (DCP)
NYC Board of Standards and Appeals (BSA)
NYC Department of Buildings (DOB)
NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC)
correctOption: NYC Department of Buildings (DOB)
explanation: The NYC Department of Buildings (DOB) enforces the NYC Zoning Resolution as it applies to individual building permit applications. The Department of City Planning (DCP) administers and amends the zoning text. The BSA hears variance and special permit applications, and the LPC regulates landmark properties.
codeReference: NYC Building Code 2022 S.28-101; NYC Charter S.643

question: NYC Local Law 97 of 2019 primarily targets which building performance metric?
options: Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from large buildings
Water consumption in high-rise residential buildings
Structural safety of aging building facades
Elevator inspection and modernization requirements
correctOption: Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from large buildings
explanation: NYC Local Law 97 (2019) sets mandatory greenhouse gas emissions caps for most buildings over 25,000 SF, with compliance periods beginning 2024 and tightening in 2030. Buildings exceeding their limits face substantial annual penalties. It is part of NYC's Climate Mobilization Act aiming for carbon neutrality by 2050.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 97 of 2019; NYC Administrative Code S.28-320

question: Under the NYC Zoning Resolution, what is a 'non-conforming building' (as opposed to a non-conforming use)?
options: A building that was legally constructed but does not conform to current bulk regulations (height, setback, FAR)
A building that violates structural safety requirements
A building operating a use not permitted in its zoning district
A building that has not received a certificate of occupancy
correctOption: A building that was legally constructed but does not conform to current bulk regulations (height, setback, FAR)
explanation: Under NYC Zoning Resolution Article V, a non-conforming building is one legally established but not conforming to current bulk regulations (height, setbacks, FAR, lot coverage). A non-conforming use occupies a space with a use not permitted in the current district. The two are distinct - a building can be non-conforming in bulk but conforming in use, or vice versa.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Article V, Chapter 5; S.52-00 et seq.

question: NYC Local Law 11 (Facade Inspection Safety Program - FISP) requires periodic exterior facade inspections for buildings over how many stories?
options: 10 stories
4 stories
6 stories
8 stories
correctOption: 6 stories
explanation: NYC Local Law 11 (codified as FISP - Facade Inspection Safety Program, NYC Admin Code S.28-302) requires buildings with more than 6 stories to have their exterior walls and appurtenances inspected by a qualified exterior wall inspector (QEWI) every 5 years. Reports are filed with the DOB classifying conditions as Safe, Safe with Repair & Maintenance Program (SWARMP), or Unsafe.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code S.28-302; NYC Local Law 11 of 1998 (FISP)

question: Under NYC Building Code 2022, which document certifies that a building complies with all applicable laws and is safe for occupancy?
options: Letter of Completion (LC)
Notice of Approval from the DOB Plan Examiner
Certificate of Occupancy (CO)
Temporary Certificate of Occupancy (TCO)
correctOption: Certificate of Occupancy (CO)
explanation: A Certificate of Occupancy (CO) is issued by the NYC DOB after all required inspections confirm a new building or changed occupancy complies with the Building Code, Zoning Resolution, and other applicable laws. A Temporary Certificate of Occupancy (TCO) allows partial occupancy while minor work is completed. A Letter of Completion is issued for permitted work not changing occupancy (e.g., interior renovation).
codeReference: NYC Building Code 2022 S.28-118; NYC Administrative Code

question: The NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC) reviews proposed changes to designated landmark buildings. Which type of approval is required for a minor repair using the same materials and methods as the original construction?
options: Certificate of No Effect (CNE)
Permit for Minor Work (PMW)
Certificate of Appropriateness (CoA)
No LPC approval is required for like-for-like repairs
correctOption: Permit for Minor Work (PMW)
explanation: The LPC issues a Permit for Minor Work (PMW) for in-kind repairs using the same materials and methods as the existing landmark (NYC Admin Code §25-310). A Certificate of No Effect (CNE) applies when proposed work is more involved but will not affect any protected architectural features. A Certificate of Appropriateness (CoA) is required for significant alterations that could affect the landmark's character.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code S.25-306 et seq.; NYC LPC Rules (63 RCNY Chapter 2)

question: NYC Local Law 26 of 2004 requires the installation of sprinkler systems in existing high-rise office buildings. What height defines a 'high-rise' building in NYC for this purpose?
options: Over 100 feet in height
Over 150 feet in height
Over 75 feet in height
Over 6 stories or 75 feet
correctOption: Over 100 feet in height
explanation: NYC Local Law 26 of 2004 applied its retroactive sprinkler requirement to existing office buildings 100 feet or more in height, with final compliance documentation due July 1, 2019. This 100-foot threshold is specific to the Local Law 26 sprinkler retrofit rule and should not be confused with other high-rise definitions that use a 75-foot occupied-floor trigger.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 26 of 2004; NYC Administrative Code former Section 27-929.1; NYC DOB Local Law 26 sprinkler guidance

question: Under the NYC Building Code, which professionals may self-certify their permit applications through the DOB Professional Certification program?
options: Architects and engineers enrolled in the DOB Professional Certification (ProfCert) program
Identify required inspections, responsible agencies, and filing obligations before work begins
General contractors with a NYC Master License
Any licensed architect or engineer registered in New York State
correctOption: Architects and engineers enrolled in the DOB Professional Certification (ProfCert) program
explanation: NYC DOB's Professional Certification (ProfCert) program allows pre-approved, enrolled licensed architects and engineers to self-certify permit applications for code compliance, bypassing standard plan examination queues. In exchange, ProfCert participants are subject to random DOB audits and bear greater personal responsibility for compliance. Enrollment and maintained good standing are required.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code Section 28-104.2.1; NYC DOB Professional Certification Program Rules (1 RCNY Section 101-03)

question: What is the purpose of NYC's Special Inspection Program during construction?
options: To replace the architect's site observations required under AIA A201
To inspect contractor safety equipment before work begins
To provide independent third-party verification of critical structural and life-safety work beyond standard DOB inspections
To certify the building for LEED or energy code compliance
correctOption: To provide independent third-party verification of critical structural and life-safety work beyond standard DOB inspections
explanation: NYC's Special Inspection Program (based on IBC Chapter 17) requires a registered Special Inspection Agency (SIA) hired by the owner to verify critical work -- concrete, masonry, steel connections, high-strength bolting, pile driving, spray-applied fireproofing -- during and after construction. The SIA provides independent oversight beyond DOB periodic inspections.
codeReference: NYC Building Code 2022 Chapter 17; IBC 2015 Chapter 17; NCARB ARE 5.0 CE Guidelines

question: NYC Local Law 84 requires buildings over 25,000 SF to annually report energy consumption using which platform?
options: ASHRAE Energy Audit Reporting System
NYC DOB Building Energy Data Portal
EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager
LEED Energy Performance Dashboard
correctOption: EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager
explanation: NYC Local Law 84 (2009), expanded by subsequent legislation, requires annual benchmarking and public disclosure of energy and water consumption for buildings over 25,000 SF using EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager. The data informs Local Law 97 carbon emissions penalties and supports owners in identifying energy efficiency improvement opportunities.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 84 of 2009; NYC Administrative Code Section 28-309; EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager

question: In NYC, what is the Board of Standards and Appeals (BSA) variance used for?
options: To challenge an Environmental Impact Statement finding
To obtain landmark designation for a historic building
To appeal a DOB rejection based on Building Code interpretation
To obtain relief from strict Zoning Resolution requirements causing practical difficulty or undue hardship
correctOption: To obtain relief from strict Zoning Resolution requirements causing practical difficulty or undue hardship
explanation: The NYC Board of Standards and Appeals (BSA) hears applications for variances from the Zoning Resolution when strict compliance would cause practical difficulty (area variances) or undue hardship (use variances) due to unique site conditions. Variances are not granted automatically -- applicants must demonstrate specific hardship. The BSA also hears Building Code appeals.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Section 72-21; NYC Charter Sections 666-668; NYC Administrative Code Section 28-103.3

question: NYC Local Law 196 of 2017 expanded which construction safety requirement?
options: Site Safety Training (SST) requiring workers and supervisors to complete OSHA 10/30-hour training plus site-specific training
Mandatory licensing for all subcontractors on jobs over $5 million
Weekly safety inspections by a licensed site safety manager
Mandatory drug testing for all construction workers
correctOption: Site Safety Training (SST) requiring workers and supervisors to complete OSHA 10/30-hour training plus site-specific training
explanation: NYC Local Law 196 of 2017 requires workers on most covered NYC construction sites to obtain a 40-hour Site Safety Training (SST) card issued by a DOB-approved trainer. Site Safety Supervisors must complete a 62-hour SST card. These programs incorporate OSHA 10 content (within the 40-hour track) and OSHA 30 content (within the 62-hour track). Cards are issued by DOB-approved training providers — not directly by OSHA.
codeReference: NYC Local Law 196 of 2017; NYC Administrative Code Section 28-408; NYC DOB SST Requirements

question: On a NYC project requiring special inspections, who typically retains the registered Special Inspection Agency?
options: The DOB plan examiner
The construction worker performing the inspected work
The owner
The building product manufacturer
correctOption: The owner
explanation: NYC special inspections are performed by qualified special inspectors working for registered Special Inspection Agencies. The owner is responsible for retaining the required special inspection agency, and responsibilities are documented through DOB technical reports such as the TR1.
codeReference: NYC Building Code 2022 Chapter 17; NYC Administrative Code Section 28-115; NYC DOB Special Inspections

question: A NYC renovation will occur while portions of the building remain occupied. What document is typically required to describe how occupants will be protected during construction?
options: Architect's supplemental instruction
LEED scorecard
Tenant Protection Plan
Certificate of Substantial Completion
correctOption: Tenant Protection Plan
explanation: For certain work in occupied buildings, NYC requires a Tenant Protection Plan addressing measures such as egress, fire safety, health requirements, dust control, noise, and essential services during construction.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code Section 28-104.8.4; NYC Building Code 2022 Chapter 33; NYC DOB Tenant Protection Plan requirements

question: A NYC permit application involves work in a building with occupied dwelling units. When is a Tenant Protection Plan typically required?
options: Limit the plan to contractor logistics outside the building
Address egress, fire safety, dust, noise, services, and construction impacts on occupants
When a construction, alteration, or partial demolition permit affects a building with at least one occupied dwelling unit
Treat occupied residential work as exempt from DOB safeguards
correctOption: When a construction, alteration, or partial demolition permit affects a building with at least one occupied dwelling unit
explanation: NYC DOB requires a Tenant Protection Plan for certain permitted work when tenants remain in occupied dwelling units. The plan addresses tenant protections during construction.
codeReference: NYC DOB Tenant Protection Plan requirements; NYC Administrative Code Section 28-104.8.4

question: Which topic is specifically addressed in a NYC Tenant Protection Plan?
options: Address egress, fire safety, dust, noise, services, and construction impacts on occupants
Calculating architect profit rate
Maintaining adequate egress and fire safety during construction
Selecting the owner interior finishes
correctOption: Maintaining adequate egress and fire safety during construction
explanation: A TPP addresses protections such as egress, fire safety, health requirements, dust control, noise, and essential services during construction.
codeReference: NYC DOB Tenant Protection Plan requirements; NYC Building Code 2022 Chapter 33

question: Under NYC FISP requirements, which buildings generally require periodic exterior wall inspections?
options: Buildings undergoing exterior repair during the current calendar year
Buildings taller than 75 feet regardless of story count
Buildings higher than six stories
Buildings with any street-facing masonry wall
correctOption: Buildings higher than six stories
explanation: NYC FISP applies to buildings higher than six stories and requires periodic exterior wall inspection reports by qualified professionals.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code Section 28-302; NYC DOB Facade Inspection & Safety Program

question: A NYC FISP report classifies a facade as unsafe. What is the general implication?
options: The owner may defer repairs until a future capital project if sidewalk protection is installed
The condition threatens public safety and requires prompt protection and repair according to DOB requirements
The facade is safe but must be reinspected before the next five-year cycle
The condition is treated as SWARMP if no pieces have fallen
correctOption: The condition threatens public safety and requires prompt protection and repair according to DOB requirements
explanation: Unsafe FISP conditions indicate a public-safety concern requiring protective measures and correction under DOB rules and filings.

question: Who performs NYC FISP inspections for facade compliance reports?
options: A Qualified Exterior Wall Inspector retained by the owner
A DOB plan examiner assigned after the owner files the report
The contractor performing the facade repair work
A building superintendent documenting facade conditions for the owner
correctOption: A Qualified Exterior Wall Inspector retained by the owner
explanation: FISP inspections are performed by qualified registered design professionals, commonly QEWIs, retained by building owners to inspect and file required reports.

question: Which NYC code chapter most directly governs safeguards during construction or demolition, such as public protection and site safety measures?

NYC Building Code Chapter 3

NYC Building Code Chapter 33
correctOption: NYC Building Code Chapter 33
explanation: NYC Building Code Chapter 33 addresses safeguards during construction or demolition, including protections for the public and adjacent property.

question: A NYC project requires special inspections. What is the purpose of the TR1 form in the DOB filing process?

Identify required inspections, responsible agencies, and filing obligations before work begins
To approve zoning variances
To identify required special and progress inspections and the responsible inspection agencies or professionals
correctOption: To identify required special and progress inspections and the responsible inspection agencies or professionals
explanation: The TR1 identifies required special or progress inspections and documents the responsible parties for those inspections in the DOB filing process.

question: A registered design professional professionally certifies a DOB filing. What risk remains?

DOB may audit the filing and the professional remains responsible for code compliance
DOB cannot ever review the filing
correctOption: DOB may audit the filing and the professional remains responsible for code compliance
explanation: Professional certification can speed review, but it does not remove the professional responsibility for code compliance or DOB audit authority.

question: Which NYC agency reviews proposed exterior work affecting designated landmark buildings?

NYC Department of Finance only

question: A building owner wants to combine adjacent zoning lots to use available floor area across parcels. What NYC zoning concept is involved?

question: A NYC building subject to Local Law 97 exceeds its annual emissions limit. What is the main regulatory concern?

A required change from residential to factory occupancy
Automatic waiver of all energy requirements

explanation: Local Law 97 establishes greenhouse gas emissions limits for many large buildings and can impose penalties for noncompliance.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code Section 28-320; NYC Local Law 97 of 2019

question: Which platform is commonly used for annual NYC building energy benchmarking required by Local Law 84?

DOB NOW Build job filing portal
AIA G704 Certificate of Substantial Completion
NYC Zoning and Land Use map

explanation: NYC benchmarking uses ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager for reporting energy and water data for covered buildings.

Only final art placement
Only room names
Insulation, fenestration U-factor/SHGC, air leakage, commissioning, and compliance path

question: When coordinating NYC special inspections, which action best manages risk when DOB objections conflict with the design team's interpretation?
options: Build before approval
Ask the contractor to negotiate informally
Document the objection, research code basis, coordinate response, and revise or request reconsideration as appropriate
Ignore the objection
correctOption: Document the objection, research code basis, coordinate response, and revise or request reconsideration as appropriate
explanation: DOB objections require documented code analysis and coordinated response before approval can proceed.

options: Build before approval
Ask the contractor to negotiate informally
Document the objection, research code basis, coordinate response, and revise or request reconsideration as appropriate
Ignore the objection

question: For an alteration in a flood-prone NYC site, which NYC requirement is most relevant to firestopping penetrations in rated assemblies?
options: Maintaining tested fire-resistance ratings at penetrations and joints
Furniture budget
FAR calculation only
Marketing plan

options: Marketing plan
Furniture budget

Maintaining tested fire-resistance ratings at penetrations and joints

Only new-construction rules without existing-building provisions
NYC Existing Building Code work classification and triggered compliance requirements
Only contractor preference

explanation: Existing-building codes determine how alterations, repairs, additions, and changes of occupancy are regulated.

options: Only new-construction rules without existing-building provisions

NYC Existing Building Code work classification and triggered compliance requirements
Only final photography

Delay service planning until move-in
Coordinate zoning/loading requirements, sanitation needs, curb access, service circulation, and tenant operations
Eliminate service access

explanation: Dense urban projects require early service planning to avoid operational and regulatory conflicts.

Sprinkler, alarm, egress, compartmentation, fire ratings, and occupancy requirements
Only mailbox color

Only lobby furniture

options: Only project renderings
Only final cleaning
Only furniture procurement
Confirm DOB/sidewalk shed requirements, public protection, permits, and site safety responsibilities

explanation: NYC construction work often requires pedestrian protection and site-safety coordination.

question: During NYC zoning feasibility review, which factor is most important when evaluating a landmarked NYC property?
options: Landmarks Preservation Commission approval in addition to DOB and code requirements
Only furniture finish
Only the project hashtag
Only contractor preference

question: For a Manhattan mixed-use building, which factor is most important when evaluating a landmarked NYC property?
options: Landmarks Preservation Commission approval in addition to DOB and code requirements
Only furniture finish

Only the project hashtag

explanation: Landmarked properties may require LPC approval that affects exterior work, materials, windows, signage, and timing.

question: For a Manhattan mixed-use building, which NYC requirement is most directly related to inspecting energy-code installation before sign-off?
options: Required progress inspections for energy-code items
Marketing approval
Furniture punch list
AIA G704 only

explanation: NYC energy compliance includes required inspections confirming installed work matches approved energy documents.

question: When evaluating an existing NYC facade, which NYC requirement is most directly related to inspecting energy-code installation before sign-off?
options: Marketing approval
Required progress inspections for energy-code items

question: When evaluating an existing NYC facade, which early check best reduces risk for an eating-and-drinking establishment in NYC?
options: Confirm zoning use, occupant load, accessibility, exhaust, grease waste, fire protection, and agency approvals
Delay exhaust until after lease signing
Assume prior tenant approval transfers automatically
Select chairs first
correctOption: Confirm zoning use, occupant load, accessibility, exhaust, grease waste, fire protection, and agency approvals
explanation: Restaurant projects combine zoning, building, mechanical, health, accessibility, and fire-safety constraints.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution; 2022 NYC Building Code; NYC Mechanical Code

question: For a building subject to Local Law 97, which early check best reduces risk for an eating-and-drinking establishment in NYC?
options: Delay exhaust until after lease signing
Confirm zoning use, occupant load, accessibility, exhaust, grease waste, fire protection, and agency approvals
Select chairs first
Assume prior tenant approval transfers automatically
correctOption: Confirm zoning use, occupant load, accessibility, exhaust, grease waste, fire protection, and agency approvals
explanation: Restaurant projects combine zoning, building, mechanical, health, accessibility, and fire-safety constraints.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution; 2022 NYC Building Code; NYC Mechanical Code

question: For a building subject to Local Law 97, which action is best when an owner asks to start NYC work before permit issuance?
options: Explain permit requirements, enforcement risk, and legal sequence before construction starts
Label the work as maintenance regardless of scope
Start work quietly
Ask the contractor to decide
correctOption: Explain permit requirements, enforcement risk, and legal sequence before construction starts
explanation: Unpermitted work can create violations, stop-work orders, penalties, and safety risks.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code Title 28; NYC DOB permit requirements

question: When coordinating NYC special inspections, which action is best when an owner asks to start NYC work before permit issuance?
options: Ask the contractor to decide
Start work quietly
Label the work as maintenance regardless of scope
Explain permit requirements, enforcement risk, and legal sequence before construction starts
correctOption: Explain permit requirements, enforcement risk, and legal sequence before construction starts
explanation: Unpermitted work can create violations, stop-work orders, penalties, and safety risks.
codeReference: NYC Administrative Code Title 28; NYC DOB permit requirements

question: Which NYC agency issues building permits and enforces the NYC Building Code?
options: Fire Department of New York (FDNY)
Department of Buildings (DOB)
Board of Standards and Appeals (BSA)
Department of City Planning (DCP)
correctOption: Department of Buildings (DOB)
explanation: The NYC Department of Buildings (DOB) administers and enforces the NYC Building Code, Zoning Resolution (in coordination with DCP), and related laws. It issues permits, conducts inspections, and enforces violations for all private building work.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code Section 28-101; NYC Charter Section 643

question: In NYC, what does FAR stand for and what does it control?
options: Fire Area Rating — the fire-resistance rating of a building assembly
Foundation Allowable Resistance — the bearing capacity of the soil
Floor Area Ratio — the ratio of total building floor area to the zoning lot area, limiting total building size
Floor-to-Attic Ratio — the proportion of usable vs. mechanical space
correctOption: Floor Area Ratio — the ratio of total building floor area to the zoning lot area, limiting total building size
explanation: In the NYC Zoning Resolution, FAR (Floor Area Ratio) is the primary tool for controlling building bulk. A lot with a FAR of 3.0 and 10,000 SF area can have a maximum of 30,000 SF of zoning floor area, on any number of floors.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Section 12-10; ZR Section 23-22

question: What is the purpose of NYC Zoning Resolution Section 72-21 (BSA Variance)?
options: To grant landmark designation
To issue building permits for large developments
To grant relief from strict Zoning Resolution requirements when they cause undue hardship due to unique site conditions
To approve environmental impact statements
correctOption: To grant relief from strict Zoning Resolution requirements when they cause undue hardship due to unique site conditions
explanation: The Board of Standards and Appeals (BSA) can grant a use or area variance under ZR Section 72-21 when strict application of the zoning would create an undue hardship due to unique physical conditions of the lot—not just financial difficulty.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Section 72-21; NYC Charter Section 666

question: What is a Certificate of Occupancy (CO) in NYC?
options: A document issued by the DOB certifying that a building complies with applicable codes and may be legally occupied for its approved use
A permit required before excavation begins
A document approving exterior renovation only
A zoning variance granted by the BSA
correctOption: A document issued by the DOB certifying that a building complies with applicable codes and may be legally occupied for its approved use
explanation: In NYC, a Certificate of Occupancy (CO) is required before a new building or a building with a change of use can be legally occupied. It certifies that construction conforms to approved plans and applicable codes. Temporary COs may be issued for phased occupancy.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code Section 28-118; NYC Administrative Code Section 28-118.1

question: Under the NYC Zoning Resolution, what is the primary difference between a 'use variance' and a 'special permit'?
options: A use variance relieves undue hardship from unique site conditions; a special permit allows uses that are conditionally acceptable if design standards are met
A use variance allows any use; a special permit is only for retail
They are the same thing
A special permit is always approved by the BSA; a use variance by DCP
correctOption: A use variance relieves undue hardship from unique site conditions; a special permit allows uses that are conditionally acceptable if design standards are met
explanation: A use variance (ZR 72-21) requires proving undue hardship from unique physical conditions. A special permit (ZR 73-00) is an as-of-right pathway for certain uses that the Zoning Resolution permits conditionally, subject to design and operational standards, approved by the BSA or City Planning Commission.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Section 72-21 and 73-00; NYC Charter Section 666

question: What does the NYC Building Code require before any work can begin on a building?
options: A work permit issued by the DOB (except for minor work exempt by code)
Only a zoning approval from DCP
An approved Environmental Impact Statement
Only a landmark approval from LPC if in a historic district
correctOption: A work permit issued by the DOB (except for minor work exempt by code)
explanation: NYC Building Code Section 28-105 requires a permit before most construction work begins. Minor repairs and ordinary maintenance may be exempt, but new construction, alterations, and demolition require a DOB work permit with approved plans.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code Section 28-105; NYC Administrative Code Section 28-105.1

question: Under the 2022 NYC Building Code, which occupancy group applies to a multi-tenant office building?
options: Group M (Mercantile)
Group A-2 (Assembly)
Group B (Business)
Group R-2 (Residential)
correctOption: Group B (Business)
explanation: NYC BC Section 304 follows IBC occupancy classifications. A multi-tenant office building falls under Group B (Business). Occupancy classification drives required means of egress, fire protection systems, construction type, and allowable height/area.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code Section 304; IBC 2015 Section 304

question: What is the minimum ADA-compliant door clear width under the NYC Building Code and 2010 ADA Standards?
options: 44 inches clear
36 inches clear
32 inches clear (measured when the door is open 90 degrees)
24 inches clear
correctOption: 32 inches clear (measured when the door is open 90 degrees)
explanation: ADA Standards Section 404.2.3 and NYC BC Section 1101 require a minimum clear opening width of 32 inches for accessible doors (door leaf open 90 degrees). A 36-inch door leaf provides this clearance. The preferred accessible door width is 36 inches clear for easier wheelchair passage.
codeReference: 2010 ADA Standards Section 404.2.3; 2022 NYC Building Code Section 1101

question: What is the primary purpose of NYC Local Law 11 (Facade Inspection Safety Program, FISP)?
options: To require fire sprinkler retrofits in existing buildings
To require periodic inspection and repair of exterior walls and appurtenances of buildings over 6 stories to ensure public safety
To mandate annual energy audits
To regulate window installation standards
correctOption: To require periodic inspection and repair of exterior walls and appurtenances of buildings over 6 stories to ensure public safety
explanation: NYC Local Law 11 (now part of the Facade Inspection Safety Program, FISP) requires owners of buildings taller than 6 stories to have qualified professionals inspect exterior walls every 5 years and repair any unsafe conditions. It was enacted after a 1979 facade collapse killed a student.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code Chapter 28-302; NYC Administrative Code S.28-302.1

question: Under the NYC Zoning Resolution, what is a 'use group'?
options: A cluster of zoning lots under common ownership
A classification grouping similar types of land uses (residential, commercial, manufacturing) that determines where activities are permitted in different zoning districts
A category of construction types based on fire resistance
A group of building owners in the same neighborhood
correctOption: A classification grouping similar types of land uses (residential, commercial, manufacturing) that determines where activities are permitted in different zoning districts
explanation: The NYC Zoning Resolution organizes land uses into Use Groups (UG 1–18) based on their character and compatibility. Each zoning district (R, C, M) permits specific Use Groups, controlling what activities can legally operate in each area of the city.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution Section 22-11; ZR Article II Chapter 2

question: What minimum fire-resistance rating does the NYC Building Code require for floor assemblies in Type I-A construction?
options: No rating required
1 hour
2 hours
3 hours
correctOption: 3 hours
explanation: IBC Table 601 (adopted by NYC BC) requires Type I-A (the most fire-resistant construction) to have floor assemblies with a minimum 3-hour fire-resistance rating. Type I-A is used for tall buildings where compartmentalization and structural fire resistance are critical.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code Table 601; IBC 2015 Table 601

question: Under the NYC Energy Conservation Code, which standard sets minimum energy efficiency requirements for new commercial buildings?
options: ASHRAE 62.1
IBC Chapter 35 only
ASHRAE 90.1
IECC residential only
correctOption: ASHRAE 90.1
explanation: The NYC Energy Conservation Code for commercial buildings is based on ASHRAE Standard 90.1 (Energy Standard for Sites and Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings). NYC has adopted and amended this standard for local requirements.
codeReference: NYC Energy Conservation Code (ECCC) 2020; ASHRAE 90.1-2019

question: What is a 'Special Inspection' in the NYC Building Code?
options: A homeowner's inspection before purchase
The DOB's routine permit inspection
Inspection by an approved agency to verify that certain critical work conforms to approved construction documents and code requirements
An inspection required only for landmarks
correctOption: Inspection by an approved agency to verify that certain critical work conforms to approved construction documents and code requirements
explanation: Special Inspections (NYC BC Chapter 17 / IBC Chapter 17) are required for critical structural and fire-protection work—concrete, structural steel, masonry, spray-applied fireproofing, pile foundations. They are performed by an approved special inspector, separate from regular DOB inspection.
codeReference: 2022 NYC Building Code Section 1705; IBC 2015 Chapter 17

question: What is the primary tool the NYC Department of City Planning uses to regulate land use across all five boroughs?
options: The Zoning Resolution
The NYC Charter
The Environmental Review Process (CEQR)
The Building Code
correctOption: The Zoning Resolution
explanation: The NYC Zoning Resolution, administered by the Department of City Planning, regulates land use (what can be built where), density (FAR), bulk (height, setbacks, lot coverage), and parking across all five boroughs. It is distinct from the Building Code, which governs how buildings are constructed.
codeReference: NYC Zoning Resolution (amended through 2024); NYC Charter Section 200

question: Under NYC accessibility requirements, what is the maximum slope of a sidewalk accessible route before it is classified as a ramp requiring handrails?
explanation: ADA Standards Section 402.2 classifies a surface with a slope greater than 1:20 (5%) as a ramp. Slopes at or below 1:20 are considered accessible walking surfaces and do not require handrails. A 1:12 slope is the maximum for a ramp.
codeReference: 2010 ADA Standards Section 402.2 and 405.2; 2022 NYC Building Code Section 1101

question: Which AIA Code of Ethics Canon primarily governs an architect's obligation to remain competent in their area of practice?
options: Canon III – Obligations to the Client
Canon I – General Obligations
Canon II – Obligations to the Public
Canon IV – Obligations to the Profession
correctOption: Canon I – General Obligations
explanation: Canon I addresses general obligations including competence. Rule 1.401 states that architects shall practice only in areas where they are competent, and Rule 1.402 requires them to keep current with developments in the profession. Canon II covers professional conduct more broadly, not competence specifically.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon I Rule 1.401; NCARB PcM Objective 1.1

question: An architect is asked to seal a set of structural drawings prepared entirely by an engineering firm without the architect's involvement. Which action is correct?
options: Decline to seal drawings not prepared under the architect's direct supervision
Seal the drawings if the owner provides written authorization
Seal the drawings after reviewing them for coordination
Seal the drawings because the engineer is a licensed professional
correctOption: Decline to seal drawings not prepared under the architect's direct supervision
explanation: An architect may not affix their seal to documents not prepared under their direct supervision. Doing so misrepresents the architect's professional involvement and violates both AIA ethics rules and state licensing laws. The engineer of record should seal their own structural drawings.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon II Rule 2.103; NCARB PcM Objective 1.2

question: What is the standard of care against which an architect's performance is measured in a negligence claim?
options: Compliance with all manufacturer recommendations for every product specified
The highest level of skill possessed by any architect in the country
The ordinary skill and care used by competent architects in the same locality under similar circumstances
Perfection and zero defects in construction documents
correctOption: The ordinary skill and care used by competent architects in the same locality under similar circumstances
explanation: The legal standard of care for architects is the ordinary skill and care used by competent architects in the same locality under similar circumstances. It does not require perfection. This locality-based standard acknowledges that practice conditions, codes, and resources vary by region.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon I; NCARB PcM Objective 1.1

question: Under AIA B101-2017, which party owns the copyright to the instruments of service, such as drawings and specifications?
options: The owner who paid for the services
The architect, who retains ownership as instruments of service
The copyright is shared equally between the owner and architect
The contractor who builds from the documents
correctOption: The architect, who retains ownership as instruments of service
explanation: AIA B101-2017 §7.2 establishes that the architect and its consultants retain all common law, statutory, and other reserved rights, including copyright, in the instruments of service. The owner receives a limited nonexclusive license to use the documents for the specific project only.
codeReference: AIA B101-2017 §7.2; NCARB PcM Objective 4.1

question: Which type of insurance covers an employee who is injured while performing a construction site visit during working hours?
options: Workers' compensation insurance
Umbrella liability insurance
Commercial general liability (CGL) insurance
Professional liability (E&O) insurance
correctOption: Workers' compensation insurance
explanation: Workers' compensation insurance covers employees for injuries sustained in the course of employment, regardless of fault. It is required by most states including New York. Professional liability covers errors in professional services, and CGL covers third-party bodily injury and property damage, not employee injuries.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.2

question: An architect working in New York is approached by a developer client to design a 40-story mixed-use high-rise. The architect has only designed single-family residences. Which AIA Code of Ethics rule is most directly implicated?
options: Rule 5.101 – not make misleading statements about services
Rule 4.201 – disclose conflicts of interest
Rule 2.101 – uphold the integrity of the profession
Rule 1.401 – practice only in areas of competence
correctOption: Rule 1.401 – practice only in areas of competence
explanation: Rule 1.401 under Canon I states that members shall practice architecture only when qualified by education, training, or experience in the specific area of practice. Accepting a complex high-rise project without the requisite competence directly violates this rule. The architect should either associate with a qualified firm or decline the project.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon I Rule 1.401; NCARB PcM Objective 1.1

question: An architect discovers that a contractor on an active project has submitted a fraudulent payment application to the owner. The architect's contract includes construction administration services. What is the architect's most appropriate first action?
options: Notify the owner of the suspected fraud and decline to certify the application
Immediately terminate the contractor and hire a replacement
Report the contractor to the state licensing board without informing the owner
Certify the payment application to avoid delaying the project schedule
correctOption: Notify the owner of the suspected fraud and decline to certify the application
explanation: AIA Code of Ethics Canon II Rule 2.101 requires architects to uphold the integrity and dignity of the profession. Certifying a fraudulent application would make the architect complicit. The architect's primary obligation during CA is to the owner's interests; notifying the owner and withholding certification is both ethically and contractually correct under AIA B101-2017 §3.6.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon II Rule 2.101; AIA B101-2017 §3.6.4; NCARB PcM Objective 1.2

question: A client asks an architect to design an addition to a building that the architect knows is already in violation of the local zoning setback requirements. The client insists the violation is minor and wants to proceed. What should the architect do?
options: Report the client to the local building department before proceeding
Design the addition but include a disclaimer in the contract
Proceed since the existing violation is not the architect's responsibility
Advise the client of the existing violation and decline to design work that would exacerbate it without resolution
correctOption: Advise the client of the existing violation and decline to design work that would exacerbate it without resolution
explanation: Canon III Rule 3.101 requires architects to serve the public interest, which includes upholding the law. Designing an addition that compounds an existing code violation would make the architect complicit in a legal violation. The correct approach is to advise the client of the issue and require resolution — such as a variance or correction — before proceeding.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon III Rule 3.101; NCARB PcM Objective 1.3

question: An architect has a 15% ownership stake in a building materials company whose products they frequently specify. A new client hires them to design a commercial building. What is the architect's ethical obligation?
options: Disclose the financial interest to the client before specifying any of those products
Specify only competitor products to avoid any appearance of conflict
Divest from the materials company before beginning any design work
No obligation exists because ownership stakes in other companies are private financial matters
correctOption: Disclose the financial interest to the client before specifying any of those products
explanation: AIA Code of Ethics Canon IV Rule 4.201 requires members to promptly disclose potential conflicts of interest to clients. A financial stake in a company whose products are specified creates a conflict. Disclosure allows the client to make an informed decision and preserves the architect-client trust relationship. Divestiture is not required — disclosure is.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon IV Rule 4.201; NCARB PcM Objective 1.4

question: A senior architect at a firm submits a project for a national design awards competition and lists only themselves as the designer, omitting three junior architects who contributed significant design work. Which AIA rule is most directly violated?
options: Rule 5.301 – give appropriate attribution for work
Rule 6.101 – respect the rights of others
Rule 2.101 – uphold the integrity of the profession
Rule 1.402 – keep current with professional developments
correctOption: Rule 5.301 – give appropriate attribution for work
explanation: Canon V Rule 5.301 specifically requires members to recognize and give credit to those who have contributed to their work. Submitting a competition entry that omits co-contributors misrepresents authorship and directly violates this attribution obligation. It may also implicate Rule 2.103 if it constitutes a knowingly false statement.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon V Rule 5.301; NCARB PcM Objective 1.5

question: An architect publishes a social media post claiming a competitor's firm submitted falsified project budgets to win a public contract, based solely on an unverified rumor from a disgruntled former employee. Which AIA rule is most likely violated?
options: Rule 5.101 – not make false/misleading statements about own services
Rule 6.102 – not maliciously injure the professional reputation of another
Rule 3.102 – not participate in illegal discrimination
Rule 4.301 – not solicit work for which there is a conflict of interest
correctOption: Rule 6.102 – not maliciously injure the professional reputation of another
explanation: Canon VI Rule 6.102 prohibits members from making malicious statements about other architects that injure their professional reputation. Publishing unverified defamatory claims on social media constitutes malicious injury to reputation. Even if the architect believes the rumor, acting on it publicly without verification crosses this ethical line.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon VI Rule 6.102; NCARB PcM Objective 1.5

question: After AIA's National Ethics Council (NEC) investigates an architect and finds a violation, which of the following is NOT among the sanctions it can impose?
options: Termination of AIA membership
Suspension of AIA membership
Revocation of the architect's state license
Censure (public reprimand)
correctOption: Revocation of the architect's state license
explanation: The AIA National Ethics Council can issue censure, suspend AIA membership, or terminate AIA membership. However, the NEC cannot revoke a state architecture license — that authority rests exclusively with individual state licensing boards. NCARB can suspend or revoke its certificate, which is a separate credential from state licensure.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics; NCARB PcM Objective 1.6

question: An architect was hired by Owner A to design an office building. Owner B, a direct competitor of Owner A, later approaches the same architect to design a very similar office building on an adjacent parcel. Owner A has not consented. What is the architect's most ethically sound course of action?
options: Accept Owner B's commission since contracts with different clients are legally independent
Accept Owner B's commission but assign a different design team to the project
Disclose the conflict to both owners and decline Owner B's commission unless both provide written consent
Accept Owner B's commission as long as design documents for Owner A are kept confidential
correctOption: Disclose the conflict to both owners and decline Owner B's commission unless both provide written consent
explanation: Canon IV Rule 4.201 requires disclosure of potential conflicts to affected clients. Rule 4.301 states that architects shall not solicit work for a project where they know a conflict of interest exists. Designing directly competing facilities for adjacent competing parties is a classic conflict. Consent from both parties in writing is required before dual representation is permissible.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon IV Rule 4.201, Rule 4.301; NCARB PcM Objective 1.4

question: An architect is licensed in New York but not in California. A California client asks the architect to provide preliminary schematic design services for a building in Los Angeles, noting that a California-licensed architect will stamp the final documents. The New York architect agrees without disclosing their unlicensed status. Which rules are violated?
options: No violation occurs because a California architect will ultimately stamp the documents
Rule 1.401 and Rule 2.103, because the architect practiced outside licensure and failed to disclose
Rule 1.401 only, because the architect lacks competence in California code
Rule 2.103 only, because the client was misled about licensing status
correctOption: Rule 1.401 and Rule 2.103, because the architect practiced outside licensure and failed to disclose
explanation: Rule 1.401 requires practice only in areas of competence, which includes legal competence such as licensure in the applicable jurisdiction. Providing professional services in California without a California license violates this rule. Rule 2.103 is violated because the architect knowingly failed to disclose their unlicensed status, constituting a false implicit representation of qualifications to the client.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon I Rule 1.401, Canon II Rule 2.103; NCARB PcM Objective 1.1

question: A public agency announces a design competition where the evaluation criteria appear to strongly favor a firm that donated to the agency director's political campaign. An architect suspects but cannot confirm bias. What is the most appropriate response under the AIA Code of Ethics?
options: Withdraw and privately advise only competing firms known to the architect of the suspected bias
Participate only if the agency provides written assurance of fair evaluation criteria
Participate and submit the best possible design, then file a protest if not selected
Decline to participate and report the suspected bias to the appropriate oversight body
correctOption: Decline to participate and report the suspected bias to the appropriate oversight body
explanation: Canon III Rule 3.101 requires architects to serve the public interest. Participating in a procurement process the architect suspects is corrupted lends it legitimacy and may harm both the public and competing firms. The ethically correct action is to decline and report the suspected bias to the relevant oversight body such as a state inspector general or contracting authority, consistent with both Canon III and Canon II obligations.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon III Rule 3.101, Canon II Rule 2.101; NCARB PcM Objective 1.3

question: An architect's firm markets itself as a 'net-zero design specialist' and lists several projects in its portfolio as net-zero certified. A prospective client later discovers only one project achieved actual certification; the others only targeted net-zero during design but were never certified. Which Canon is primarily violated and what is the consequence risk?
options: Canon VI – injury to colleagues; risk of NEC censure only
Canon IV Rule 4.201 – undisclosed conflict; risk of contract termination
Canon V Rule 5.101 – false/misleading statements about services; risk of NEC action and potential state board discipline
Canon I Rule 1.402 – failure to keep current; risk of required continuing education
correctOption: Canon V Rule 5.101 – false/misleading statements about services; risk of NEC action and potential state board discipline
explanation: Canon V Rule 5.101 prohibits members from making false or misleading statements about their professional qualifications or the scope of their services. Misrepresenting non-certified projects as net-zero certified in marketing materials is a direct violation. Beyond NEC sanctions up to membership termination, state licensing boards may also discipline the architect for dishonest professional conduct under state law.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon V Rule 5.101; NCARB PcM Objective 1.5

question: Which type of professional liability policy requires that the policy be in force at the time the claim is filed, regardless of when the alleged error occurred?
options: Umbrella policy
Claims-made policy
Occurrence policy
Project-specific policy
correctOption: Claims-made policy
explanation: A claims-made professional liability (E&O) policy covers claims that are both made and reported while the policy is in force, regardless of when the underlying error occurred. An occurrence policy, by contrast, covers incidents that occur during the policy period regardless of when the claim is filed. Most architect E&O policies are claims-made, making tail coverage critical after policy expiration.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.1

question: An architect's E&O policy expires in December and the architect retires. A client files a negligence claim the following March for a project completed two years earlier. The architect did not purchase tail coverage. What is the most likely outcome?
options: The claim is covered under the contractor's CGL policy
The claim is covered because the project was completed while the policy was active
The claim is not covered because no policy was in force when the claim was filed
The claim is covered under the architect's umbrella policy
correctOption: The claim is not covered because no policy was in force when the claim was filed
explanation: Under a claims-made policy, coverage requires the policy to be in force when the claim is filed, not when the project was completed or the error occurred. Without tail (extended reporting period) coverage, a claim filed after policy expiration receives no coverage. This is one of the most significant risks architects face upon retirement or firm dissolution.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.1

question: A subcontractor is injured when scaffolding collapses on a project where the architect has a construction administration role. The subcontractor sues the architect's firm claiming inadequate site oversight. Which insurance would be the architect's PRIMARY defense?
options: Commercial general liability (CGL) insurance
Professional liability (E&O) insurance
Builder's risk insurance
Workers' compensation insurance
correctOption: Commercial general liability (CGL) insurance
explanation: CGL insurance covers third-party bodily injury and property damage claims arising from the architect's operations. When a third party such as a subcontractor is injured and sues the architect, CGL is the primary responding policy. Professional liability (E&O) covers errors and omissions in professional services, not bodily injury claims. Workers' comp covers only the architect's own employees.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.2

question: A contract clause requires the architect to indemnify the owner for 'any and all claims arising from the project, regardless of fault.' What specific risk does this broad-form indemnification clause create for the architect?
options: It voids the architect's professional liability coverage entirely
It exposes the architect to liability for claims caused solely by the owner's own negligence
It limits the architect's liability to the amount of the project fee
It shifts the architect's E&O deductible obligations to the owner
correctOption: It exposes the architect to liability for claims caused solely by the owner's own negligence
explanation: Broad-form indemnification requires the indemnitor (architect) to cover claims 'regardless of fault,' including claims caused entirely by the indemnitee's (owner's) own negligence. This is extremely hazardous because it can make the architect financially responsible for losses outside their control. AIA strongly recommends limiting indemnification to claims arising solely from the architect's own negligent acts or omissions.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.3

question: An owner-architect agreement contains a limitation of liability clause capping the architect's total liability at $50,000. The project fee is $200,000. A court finds the architect liable for $300,000 in damages. What is the maximum the architect must pay under this clause?
options: $300,000 — the full damage amount
$200,000 — limited to the project fee
$50,000 — limited by the contractual cap
$150,000 — the excess above the project fee
correctOption: $50,000 — limited by the contractual cap
explanation: A limitation of liability clause is an enforceable contractual provision that caps an architect's total liability at a specified dollar amount. When the contractually agreed cap is $50,000, that is the maximum recoverable regardless of the actual damage award, provided the clause is properly drafted and not voided by public policy. AIA strongly recommends including such clauses in all owner-architect agreements.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.3

question: A building defect caused by an architect's design error is discovered 12 years after project completion. The applicable statute of repose is 10 years. Can the owner still bring a legal claim against the architect?
options: Yes, because the statute of limitations begins when the damage is discovered
Yes, but only if the defect caused personal injury rather than property damage
No, because the statute of repose has expired, barring the claim entirely
No, but the owner can still pursue a claim against the general contractor
correctOption: No, because the statute of repose has expired, barring the claim entirely
explanation: A statute of repose is an absolute deadline measured from project completion — typically 7–10 years — after which no legal action may be brought regardless of when the defect was discovered. Unlike the statute of limitations, which is tolled by the discovery rule, the statute of repose is a hard cutoff. At 12 years post-completion against a 10-year repose period, the claim is permanently time-barred.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.4

question: Under AIA B101-2017, if an owner fails to make payment to the architect when due, how much written notice must the architect provide before suspending services?
options: 7 days written notice to the owner
14 days written notice approved by the project's lender
30 days written notice to the owner
No notice is required — the architect may immediately suspend services
correctOption: 7 days written notice to the owner
explanation: AIA B101-2017 §9.1 provides that if the owner fails to make payment when due, the architect may, after giving 7 days' written notice to the owner, suspend performance of services. This notice requirement protects both parties and allows the owner an opportunity to cure the payment default before services are interrupted.
codeReference: AIA B101-2017 §9.1; NCARB PcM Objective 4.3

question: An architect's firm carries a $1,000,000 E&O policy with a $25,000 deductible and a $500,000 umbrella policy. A negligence judgment totals $1,600,000. How much does the firm owe out-of-pocket?
options: $25,000 — only the deductible
$100,000 — the amount exceeding total insurance limits
$125,000 — deductible plus the uncovered excess above both policy limits
$600,000 — because the umbrella does not apply to professional liability
correctOption: $125,000 — deductible plus the uncovered excess above both policy limits
explanation: The firm pays the $25,000 deductible first. The E&O insurer pays the remaining portion up to the $1,000,000 limit: $975,000. The umbrella attaches above the $1,000,000 E&O limit and pays the next layer: $500,000. Total covered by insurance: $975,000 + $500,000 = $1,475,000. Total covered including deductible: $1,475,000 + $25,000 = $1,500,000. Judgment is $1,600,000. The firm owes the $25,000 deductible plus the $100,000 gap above the $1,500,000 total coverage = $125,000.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.1

question: A client's attorney argues that an architect's error caused a building defect discovered 6 years after project completion. The state has a 3-year statute of limitations running from discovery, and a 10-year statute of repose. The client discovered the defect 4 years ago but waited until now to file. Is the claim timely?
options: Yes — the discovery rule automatically extends the filing deadline by 2 additional years
No — statutes of repose always override statutes of limitations regardless of discovery
Yes — the 10-year statute of repose has not expired, so the claim is timely
No — the 3-year statute of limitations ran out a year ago, barring the claim
correctOption: No — the 3-year statute of limitations ran out a year ago, barring the claim
explanation: Both time bars must be satisfied. The statute of repose (10 years from completion) has not yet run at 6 years, so that bar is not the issue. However, the statute of limitations (3 years from discovery) began running 4 years ago when the client discovered the defect. Waiting 4 years to file exceeds the 3-year limitations period by 1 year. The claim is therefore time-barred by the statute of limitations even though the repose period remains open.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.4

question: An owner-architect agreement requires the architect to indemnify the owner 'from all claims arising from or related to the project.' The general contractor causes a worker injury due to the contractor's own unsafe practices. The injured worker sues the owner, who invokes the indemnification clause against the architect. The architect had no fault. What is the most likely risk outcome?
options: The architect is protected because the clause can only be enforced for the architect's own negligence
The architect may be required to defend and indemnify the owner despite having no fault, due to the broad language
The owner's CGL insurance must respond first before invoking any indemnification clause
The clause is automatically void in New York because it covers third-party contractor negligence
correctOption: The architect may be required to defend and indemnify the owner despite having no fault, due to the broad language
explanation: Broad-form indemnification language covering 'all claims arising from or related to the project' can obligate the architect to defend and indemnify the owner even for losses caused entirely by others. This is precisely why AIA strongly recommends limiting indemnification language to claims 'caused by the negligent acts or omissions' of the architect. Courts in some states enforce such broad clauses unless prohibited by anti-indemnity statutes.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 3.3

question: A firm is considering accepting a project involving a highly experimental facade system the firm has never used before. The owner insists the firm is qualified based on their general portfolio. Which combination of risk mitigation strategies is MOST appropriate under AIA ethics?
options: Accept the project after associating with a consultant who has demonstrated expertise in the system, and disclose the arrangement to the owner
Accept the project using a broad-form indemnification clause to transfer risk to the owner
Decline the project entirely — any unfamiliar system represents unacceptable professional liability
Accept the project and rely solely on the manufacturer's technical support
correctOption: Accept the project after associating with a consultant who has demonstrated expertise in the system, and disclose the arrangement to the owner
explanation: Canon I Rule 1.401 allows an architect to work in areas where they are not yet fully competent by associating with those who are. This is the preferred professional and ethical response to a competence gap. Broad-form indemnification does not address the underlying competence issue and creates additional risk exposure. Complete refusal ignores the viable option of leveraging specialized consultant expertise. Disclosure to the owner maintains transparency consistent with Canon IV.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon I Rule 1.401, Canon IV Rule 4.201; NCARB PcM Objective 1.1

question: During construction administration, the contractor submits an RFI asking whether a specific structural connection detail can be simplified to reduce cost. The architect approves a revised detail without consulting the structural engineer of record. The revised connection later fails. What is the architect's primary liability exposure?
options: Limited liability only, because RFI responses are not formal contract documents
The contractor bears full liability because they proposed the revision in the first place
None — responding to RFIs is a standard CA duty entirely within the architect's authority
Liability for negligence, because the architect approved a structural change outside their area of competence without consulting the engineer
correctOption: Liability for negligence, because the architect approved a structural change outside their area of competence without consulting the engineer
explanation: When an architect approves changes to structural elements without consulting the structural engineer of record, they take on professional liability for those decisions. Canon I Rule 1.401 requires practice only within areas of competence. Approving structural modifications without engineering review falls outside the architect's expertise and creates clear E&O exposure. RFI responses that authorize design modifications do carry professional liability weight.
codeReference: AIA Code of Ethics Canon I Rule 1.401; NCARB PcM Objective 3.1

question: A firm's total annual billings are $2,400,000. Direct labor costs are $800,000. Reimbursable expenses passed through to clients total $200,000. What is the firm's net multiplier?
explanation: Net multiplier = Net Revenue / Direct Labor. Net Revenue excludes reimbursables: $2,400,000 – $200,000 = $2,200,000. Net Multiplier = $2,200,000 / $800,000 = 2.75. Reimbursables must be excluded because they are pass-through costs and not revenue generated by professional services. The typical healthy net multiplier range is 2.5–3.5.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.2

question: A firm has direct labor of $600,000 and indirect (overhead) costs of $900,000 with no profit target. What is the firm's break-even multiplier?
explanation: Break-even multiplier = (Direct Labor + Overhead) / Direct Labor = ($600,000 + $900,000) / $600,000 = $1,500,000 / $600,000 = 2.50. This is the minimum multiplier required to cover both direct labor and overhead with no profit margin. Any multiplier above 2.50 in this scenario generates profit; any multiplier below results in a loss.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.2

question: A firm has 8 employees. In a given week, total hours worked by all staff are 320 hours, and billable hours total 205 hours. What is the firm's utilization rate, and how does it compare to the industry target?
options: 64.1% — within the typical target range of 62–68%
55.0% — below the typical target range of 62–68%
72.3% — above the typical target range of 62–68%
64.1% — below the typical target range of 68–75%
correctOption: 64.1% — within the typical target range of 62–68%
explanation: Utilization rate = Billable Hours / Total Hours = 205 / 320 = 64.1%. The industry-recognized target range for architectural firms is 62–68%. At 64.1%, the firm is performing within the healthy target range. A rate below 62% suggests too many non-billable hours; above 68% may indicate understaffing or unsustainable workloads.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.2

question: A firm has annual direct labor of $750,000 and annual indirect costs of $1,200,000. The firm targets a 15% profit margin on net revenue. What net multiplier must the firm achieve?
explanation: Break-even multiplier = (Direct Labor + Overhead) / Direct Labor = ($750,000 + $1,200,000) / $750,000 = 2.60. To achieve a 15% profit margin on net revenue, the firm must earn: Required Net Revenue = Total Costs / (1 – profit margin) = $1,950,000 / (1 – 0.15) = $1,950,000 / 0.85 = $2,294,118. Target multiplier = $2,294,118 / $750,000 = 3.06. This formula accounts for profit as a percentage of gross revenue rather than a simple cost markup.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.2

question: An architect charges a fee of 6% of construction cost on a $4,000,000 project. During construction, the owner approves scope changes that increase the contract sum by $600,000. What additional fee does the architect earn from the scope increase under a percentage-of-construction-cost agreement?
options: $60,000 — the fee percentage is increased for scope additions
$0 — the original fee is fixed regardless of approved changes
$36,000 — 6% of the $600,000 change order amount
$24,000 — 6% of a $400,000 net allowable increase only
correctOption: $36,000 — 6% of the $600,000 change order amount
explanation: Under a percentage-of-construction-cost fee structure, the architect's fee scales directly with the total construction cost. Owner-approved scope changes that increase the contract sum by $600,000 generate an additional fee: $600,000 × 6% = $36,000. This is one of the key advantages of the percentage fee type — the architect is automatically compensated for additional design work caused by scope growth.
codeReference: AIA B101-2017 §11.3; NCARB PcM Objective 2.1

question: An architect is hired on a cost-plus basis: all direct costs reimbursed plus 10% profit on total costs. Direct labor for the project is $120,000 and the firm's overhead rate is 150%. What total fee should the architect invoice?
explanation: Under cost-plus, all actual costs are reimbursed plus an agreed profit percentage. Overhead = $120,000 × 150% = $180,000. Total costs = $120,000 (direct labor) + $180,000 (overhead) = $300,000. Profit = $300,000 × 10% = $30,000. Total fee = $300,000 + $30,000 = $330,000. Formula: Fee = (Direct Labor + Overhead) × (1 + profit rate) = $300,000 × 1.10 = $330,000.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.1

question: An architect agreed to a stipulated-sum (fixed-fee) contract of $180,000 for a project. The project experiences significant scope creep not caused by owner-directed changes, causing the architect to spend $40,000 more in direct labor than budgeted. Under a stipulated-sum contract, who bears this cost overrun?
options: The owner, who must pay the additional $40,000 as an additional service
The contractor, whose change orders caused the scope creep
The architect, because a stipulated-sum fee is fixed regardless of actual costs
The costs are shared equally between the owner and architect by default
correctOption: The architect, because a stipulated-sum fee is fixed regardless of actual costs
explanation: A stipulated-sum (lump-sum) fee is fixed regardless of the architect's actual hours or costs. If scope creep was not caused by owner-directed changes but was instead due to underestimation by the architect, the additional labor cost falls entirely on the architect. This is the primary financial risk of the stipulated-sum fee type — inaccurate scoping directly reduces the architect's profit margin.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.1

question: A developer hires an architect on a unit-cost basis at $2,500 per residential unit for a 120-unit apartment building. During design development, the owner reduces the unit count to 95 units. What fee does the architect earn under this arrangement?
options: $237,500 — based on the revised 95-unit count
$300,000 — based on the original 120-unit count
$281,250 — prorated at 95% of the original fee
$275,000 — based on the average of original and revised unit counts
correctOption: $237,500 — based on the revised 95-unit count
explanation: Under a unit-cost fee structure, the architect's fee is directly proportional to the number of units designed. When the unit count is reduced to 95, the fee is recalculated: 95 units × $2,500/unit = $237,500. This fee type benefits the owner when scope is reduced and benefits the architect when scope grows, making contract provisions for scope reductions especially important.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.1

question: A firm earned $3,600,000 in net revenue last year. Direct labor was $1,200,000, indirect costs were $1,800,000, and reimbursable expenses passed through to clients totaled $200,000. What was the firm's profit?
explanation: Profit = Net Revenue – (Direct Labor + Overhead). Net Revenue = $3,600,000 (reimbursables are already excluded from net revenue). Total costs = $1,200,000 + $1,800,000 = $3,000,000. Profit = $3,600,000 – $3,000,000 = $600,000. The $200,000 in reimbursables is a pass-through and does not affect net revenue or profit. The profit margin is $600,000 / $3,600,000 = 16.7%.
codeReference: NCARB PcM Objective 2.2

question: Which AIA agreement form is most appropriate for a small residential addition with a straightforward, well-defined scope of work?
options: AIA B104-2017 — Standard Abbreviated Form of Agreement
AIA B102-2017 — Standard Form without Predetermined Scope of Architect's Services
AIA B108-2009 — Standard Form for Federally Funded Projects
AIA B101-2017 — Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Architect
correctOption: AIA B104-2017 — Standard Abbreviated Form of Agreement
explanation: AIA B104-2017 is specifically designed for smaller, less complex projects with straightforward scopes such as residential additions. AIA B101 is the full standard form used for larger or more complex projects. AIA B102 is used when the scope of services is not yet determined at contracting. AIA B108 is reserved for federally funded projects with additional public accountability requirements.
codeReference: AIA B104-2017; NCARB PcM Objective 4.1
